item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israeli Sniper Shoots Child in the Face, Israeli Settlers Torch Mosques
item.description: The video is hard to watch. Little Zeina pleading over her father’s body, “Father, father, my beloved father, come to me…. Why did you leave me? Why?” Zeina, of course, knows her father didn’t leave her. He and her brother were taken from her by Israel. They were killed while collecting water.…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The video is hard to watch. Little Zeina pleading over her father’s body, “Father, father, my beloved father, come to me…. Why did you leave me? Why?”

Zeina, of course, knows her father didn’t leave her. He and her brother were taken from her by Israel. They were killed while collecting water.

Zeina’s father, Mohammad Al-Habeel, and her brother, 5-year-old Mousa, are two of the more than 1,000 Palestinians killed since the so-called “ceasefire” agreement last October. “What ceasefire?” as Zeteo contributor Diana Buttu recently asked.

This week also saw more settler violence, including arson attacks on mosques, and another Palestinian child was shot in the face and killed by Israeli forces.

Here’s a brief look at another troubling week in Palestine:

Israeli settlers stole Palestinian water pipes and attacked several villages across the occupied West Bank, Palestinian media reported. The pipes that were stolen in the Khalayel Al-Loz area in the southern West Bank provided drinking water to Palestinian residents.

In Wadi Rahhal, south of Bethlehem, settlers reportedly attacked employees of the Jerusalem District Electricity Company while they were working in the area, causing minor injuries and extensive damage to their vehicle.

In the village of Jit, settlers reportedly torched Palestinian farmland and four vehicles, causing significant damage.

Israel killed 3-year-old Rayan Abu al-Ajeen, shooting him in the head, as his father carried him in central Gaza, Al Jazeera reported. His father was injured.

“My grandson, Rayan, was killed by a gunshot to the head; the bullet entered his head and exited through his eye,” Jaber Abu al-Ajeen, Rayan’s grandfather, told Al Jazeera.

The UK Court of Appeals ruled that the government’s decision to ban Palestine Action under the Terrorism Act was lawful, overturning a High Court ruling that found the proscription of the direct action group was unlawful.

Israel took over administrative control of Hebron’s holy sites, including the Ibrahimi Mosque, in violation of the 1997 Hebron Agreement, which gave the Palestinian-run Hebron Municipality planning and construction powers over the sites.

The Ibrahimi Mosque, seen as the resting place of the prophet Abraham, is significant to Muslims, Jews, and Christians alike.

The death toll from nearly-daily Israeli strikes in Gaza since the so-called “ceasefire” topped 1,000, the Palestinian Health Ministry said. That means, on average, four Palestinians have been killed each day since October.

According to Haaretz, the toll includes more than 150 children and over 120 women.

The leaders of eight Muslim-majority countries issued a statement, saying they hold Israel responsible for the rising settler violence in the occupied West Bank, including two recent arson attacks on mosques.

Palestinians say Israeli settlers set fire to mosques in the villages of Jiljiliya and Mazari a-Nubani early Wednesday, causing extensive damage. The settlers also defaced the buildings with graffiti reading “revenge” and “night of the mosques.”

Similar arson attacks took place earlier in the week.

Israel has killed one child, on average, every day during the ceasefire, UNICEF spokesperson James Elder said at a briefing.

“Let us be clear about what this means. These children were not killed in a warzone. They were killed in their homes. In their schools,” Elder said. Playing football. Fishing. They were shot, bombed, and struck by quadcopters.”

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Trump Admin Tries to Jump on Team USA World Cup Bandwagon… But Makes Racist Fool of Itself
item.description: The 2026 World Cup in the United States is off to an unmistakably racist start . A Somali referee barred. An Iraqi player reportedly interrogated for seven hours. Uzbek players patted down and aggressively screened outside the stadium. And then there is Iran: exiled to Mexico, despite all three of…
item.author: Mehdi from Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The 2026 World Cup in the United States is off to an unmistakably racist start. A Somali referee barred. An Iraqi player reportedly interrogated for seven hours. Uzbek players patted down and aggressively screened outside the stadium. And then there is Iran: exiled to Mexico, despite all three of its group-stage matches being played in the United States. “Our team is the most oppressed one in the whole World Cup,” Iran head coach Amir Ghalenoei said earlier this week.

On Friday, the Trump administration took that racism and xenophobia to the next level, but, thankfully, made a fool of itself in the process.

The groypers apparently moonlighting as the Department of Homeland Security’s social media team tried to jump on the World Cup bandwagon with this far-right, anti-immigrant tweet, ahead of the USMNT’s clash with Australia:

Got that? “Our soil. Defend the homeland. One nation. One homeland. One team.”

First, what a bizarre slogan for a host nation of the World Cup to use while describing a home game: “Defend the Homeland”? As if other teams playing at the World Cup are… invaders? Second, notice how the DHS tagline bears an uncomfortable resemblance to the Nazi slogan “Ein Volk, ein Reich, ein Führer” (“one people, one realm, one leader”). I have written elsewhere about the way in which the Trump administration’s social media accounts “can’t stop posting Nazi imagery and memes… and leaning heavily into fascist aesthetics.”

But the funniest part is that the image itself completely undermines the accompanying far-right message. It is a picture of three non-white American players (Chris Richards, Sergiño Dest, Folarin Balogun), one of whom (Balogun) scored twice in the USMNT’s 4-1 demolition of Paraguay at the start of the tournament.

Balogun is an American by virtue of.. wait for it… birthright citizenship.

The Monaco striker was born in Brooklyn in 2001, but only by accident. His parents were visiting New York from the UK, where they lived. When Balogun’s mother, seven months pregnant with a self-described “massive” belly, tried to board the return flight, she was blocked by a “concerned airline staffer.” So Balogun was born in the U.S. before returning to the UK at 2 months old.

To be clear, if the Trump administration had its way, Balogun would not be able to play for the United States. He would either be playing for England, where he was raised, or Nigeria, where his parents are from. And, in the coming weeks, if the conservative-dominated Supreme Court accepts the Trump administration’s claims and guts the 14th Amendment’s long-standing guarantee of birthright citizenship to all children born in the U.S., there will be no more Baloguns in the future.

But it isn’t just Balogun. Consider the rest of the U.S. starting lineup for Friday’s game against Australia. Four other non-white USMNT players on that pitch in Seattle also made a mockery of the dumb DHS tweet.

  • Antonee Robinson–The Fulham full-back was born in England, the son of an English mother and anaturalized American citizenfather.
  • Sergiño Dest–The PSV full-back was born in the Netherlands to a Dutch mother and aSurinamese father, who was a naturalized U.S. citizen.
  • Malik Tillman–The Bayer Leverkusen midfielder was born in Germany to an American father and aGerman mother.
  • Ricardo Pepi–The PSV striker was born in the U.S., the son ofMexican immigrant parents.

And those are just players from Friday’s starting 11.

There were also five USMNT substitutes who took to the field against Australia, three of whom have immigrant connections: Sebastian Berhalter, born in the UK, and of Puerto Rican, Polish, and German descent;

Then there is the USMNT’s star player, Christian Pulisic, nicknamed “Captain America,” who missed Friday's game against Australia due to an injury. He is, as his last name suggests, of Croatian descent.

Oh, and let’s not forget the current manager of the USMNT, Mauricio Pochettino, who is an Argentine immigrant.

So Stephen Miller can rant and rave about the threat of migrants to our culture; Donald Trump can try to block birthright citizenship; and the far-right social media team at the DHS can keep posting their neo-Nazi memes, but when it comes to the USMNT at this World Cup, it simply cannot be denied that immigrants are a blessing and diversity is our strength.

item.title: FIFA Has a Dark History of Covering for Fascists – And Trump Is No Exception
item.description: FIFA and fascism go hand-in-hand. Yes, you read that right. The organizer of the World Cup has historically been complicit in legitimizing authoritarian regimes and helping distract from their crimes – a phenomenon commonly known as “sportswashing.” Just take a look at the 1934 World Cup. And the…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
FIFA and fascism go hand-in-hand.

Yes, you read that right. The organizer of the World Cup has historically been complicit in legitimizing authoritarian regimes and helping distract from their crimes – a phenomenon commonly known as “sportswashing.”

Just take a look at the 1934 World Cup. And the 1978 World Cup… Oh, and the 2026 World Cup.

In the second episode of Zeteo’s three-part video series, “The Dark Side of the World Cup,” Mehdi walks through these three World Cups – which respectively take place under Italy’s Benito Mussolini, Argentina’s junta leader Jorge Videla, and the U.S.’s Donald Trump – to connect the dots and highlight how FIFA helped these authoritarians cover up their crimes.

Plus, Mehdi breaks down the controversies around the Russia- and Qatar-hosted World Cups and digs into the double standards when it comes to the lack of media coverage on the U.S.’s own controversies.

Paid subscribers can watch the full video above, while free subscribers can access a two-minute preview. Consider upgrading your subscription to access this full series and all Zeteo has to offer.

And if you haven’t caught up already, check out Episode 1 here.

item.title: Trump Privately Rages That Israel Is Trying to Drag Him Back Into His Iran War
item.description: Donald Trump desperately wants out of the massively unpopular war he started, and appears willing to hand Iran a stunning victory in exchange. And the U.S. president is absolutely furious at the Israeli government and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for trying to stop him from bailing on their…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump desperately wants out of the massively unpopular war he started, and appears willing to hand Iran a stunning victory in exchange. And the U.S. president is absolutely furious at the Israeli government and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for trying to stop him from bailing on their disastrous war of choice, by continuing to kill people in Lebanon despite the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding establishing a ceasefire there.

During the past several days, Trump has aggressively complained to administration officials and other confidants that the Israelis are trying to trick him into restarting a full-blown war with Iran, three people with direct knowledge of the matter and another person briefed on it tell Zeteo.

“He’s swearing a lot about it,” according to a close Trump adviser. A Trump administration official adds bluntly: “At least right now, he’s definitely madder at the Israelis than the Iranians.”

The sources who’ve spoken to President Trump in the past several days say Israel’s continued attacks in Lebanon and Israeli leaders’ efforts to pressure the Americans into abandoning the (albeit incredibly shaky) memorandum of understanding with Iran have, in fact, further driven Trump in the opposite direction.

The president keeps venting to advisers how angry he is at Netanyahu and other political and media figures – in the U.S. and in Israel – for transparently trying to drag him back into war, or for suggesting that Trump is surrendering to Iran.

The president is, however, effectively surrendering to Iran. Practically every knowledgeable observer – those opposed to the war from the jump, those who’ve been trying to start this war for decades – views the terms of the MOU as extraordinarily favorable to Iran. The deal, which Trump keeps saying is a “great deal,” would put the Iranian regime in a substantively stronger position (with a lot more cash, massive sanctions relief, and continued control over the Strait of Hormuz, for starters) than it had been in before the war began – and also help solidify its international bragging rights for fighting off the mighty war machine of the world’s undisputed superpower.

It was always clear that this war was unwinnable from its inception, and that its negotiated conclusion would never be solely on the Trump administration’s terms – and yet, Trump went ahead anyway. Trump is taking fire now from Republicans, hawkish Democrats, and much of the media for giving Iran what it wants to end this conflict, when that is the unavoidable cost of launching this incredibly stupid and avoidable war.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment on this story.

Trump, of course, is not mad at Israeli leadership out of any moral urgency or compunction. Trump, the “lazy warmonger,” illegally started a regional bloodbath, and he and his administration own the carnage and the coming years of fallout forever. And just because the Israelis, who want to keep the war going, feel betrayed by Trump, and Trump is fuming about and cursing out Netanyahu, doesn’t mean this is a permanent break in the relationship.

After all, Netanyahu and Trump have been here before. The American president spent years absolutely loathing the Israeli PM because he congratulated Joe Biden after the Democrat won the 2020 presidential election – only for Trump and Netanyahu to make up and back each other’s increasingly brutal policies. Trump has also reportedly expressed anger against Netanyahu before, in the context of Israel’s genocide in Gaza. It hasn’t meant much. Since the supposed “ceasefire” in Gaza, negotiated by Trump, Israeli forces have killed more than 1,000 people.

And just because Trump is feeling one way this week doesn’t mean he won’t redirect his wrath (or start bombing again) the following week. Since February, the U.S. commander-in-chief has vacillated so many times between waging a savage war and begging for a lasting “peace” – swinging quickly from plotting invasions and publicly threatening apocalyptic war crimes to crying uncle and pulling the plug – that it’s been easy to lose count.

What we have today is a quarrel between two like-minded, allied warmongers – it’s just that the American warmonger now seems to instinctively realize how badly his warmongering is politically destroying him and his party at home.

As Zeteo reported at the dawn of Trump’s disastrous war, virtually nobody at the highest levels of the Trump-Vance administration earnestly wanted to wage it – except for President Trump, the only one who truly matters. Basically everybody else, despite their varying degrees of queasiness, went along to get along – letting Trump know that they were with him all the way.

Now, many of them are relieved that Trump really wants to ditch the war now, meaning they’ll have more time to focus on the midterm elections and on their domestic agenda of waging war on ordinary Americans. For one thing, the number of administration officials who use the R-word when venting to Zeteo about Trump’s war is… embarrassingly substantial.

Furthermore, private polling circulated among Republicans shows that signing the MOU and Trump’s decision to try to end the war is now polling upwards of 60 percent in the U.S., a source familiar with the matter tells Zeteo. This is not, per se, because Americans broadly approve of every line or disputed detail of the memorandum; a solid majority just want Trump’s war to end ASAP, and wish it was never started in the first place!

In fact, Trump publicly acknowledged this week that his war was driving the U.S. – and global – economy into the abyss, and that he didn’t want to be a “Herbert Hoover”-type president: “I didn’t want to see economic catastrophe. If you kept this going, that could have happened. But all I know is, every time we talked about the possibility of peace, the stock market shot up like a rocket ship.”

On Wednesday, Trump called out Israel, saying he thinks they could “do better” in their conduct in Lebanon. “I’m not saying they shouldn’t protect themselves. I’m saying, when two drones are shot into the desert and drop harmlessly, you don’t have to knock down buildings in Beirut. They could behave better, and frankly they could do a better job.”

That same day, the U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding for a peace deal – ahead of a planned Friday signing – marking the biggest development toward ending Trump’s “little excursion.”

The MOU explicitly included Lebanon, reading: “The United States of America and the Islamic Republic of Iran and their allies in the current war, by signing this MOU, declared the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon, and… ensuring the territorial integrity and sovereignty of Lebanon.”

Nevertheless, Israeli leaders continued their opposition to the terms, actively continuing attacks in Lebanon. Meanwhile, many Israeli and pro-Israel officials and commentators slammed the Trump administration, aruging that it was a boon for Iran and bad for Israel. Some went as far as to attack President Trump, and even call the U.S. “treacherous.”

In response, Trump and Vance have dug their heels in, at least publicly.

In an interview with the New York Times published Thursday, Vance responded to criticism from top Israeli officials like Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich and National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir.

“What is your exact proposal? You’re a country of nine million people,” Vance said. “You can’t just kill your way out of solving every single national security problem that you have.” He pushed Israel to allow the negotiations to play out and “give a little bit of credit to the United States of America, which I think has been an incredible partner for the Israeli government for a long time.”

At a press conference at the White House the same day, Vance continued to respond sharply to Israeli critics.

“Number one: Donald J. Trump is the only head of state in the entire world who is sympathetic to the nation of Israel at this moment in time, and he happens to be the head of state of the world’s superpower,” Vance said. “If I was in the cabinet of the Israeli government, I might not be attacking the only powerful ally that I have anywhere left in the entire world.”

The vice president wasn’t freelancing; he was doing what Trump sent him out there to do. “Trump LOVED it,” a source close to the president messaged Zeteo, describing Trump’s elation at watching Vance chastise the Israeli government on TV.

Vance added: “The other thing that I would say is that over the last three months, two-thirds of the defensive weapons that have protected your homeland have been built by American hands and paid for by American tax dollars. The problem for Israel is not Donald J. Trump. And anybody in Israel who thinks their biggest problem is the President of the United States needs to wake up and smell the reality of the situation that country is in.”

And still, Israeli officials outwardly flouted the warnings, continuing to bomb Lebanon. Early Friday, Israeli forces reportedly killed 47 people and wounded another 97, while Hezbollah reportedly killed four Israeli soldiers in a tank.

Early Friday morning, the Israeli national security minister, Ben-Gvir, issued among the most horrific and dangerous statements: “For every tear of an Israeli mother, a thousand Lebanese mothers must weep. All of Lebanon must burn!”

He added: “With all due respect to the Americans, Israel must make it clear to the entire world that the blood of our sons and the security of our citizens are not forfeit. All of Lebanon must burn.”

Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz expressed similar sentiments, saying, “The entire first line of Lebanese villages has been destroyed.”

“We are destroying all the houses,” he said. “The residents will never see them standing before their eyes again.”

Israel and Hezbollah then reportedly reached a tentative ceasefire.

Iran has reportedly told mediators it stands ready to suspend the agreement and launch strikes against Israel if it does not stop attacking Lebanon. “Should the United States intervene, conditions particularly those related to the Strait of Hormuz could rapidly revert to a wartime environment,” an Iranian official told Drop Site News.

And despite that, U.S. intelligence agencies warn the Trump administration, Netanyahu is likely to undermine the peace deal by continuing Israel’s violence in Lebanon.

Do you have any info for Swin and Prem? Text them on Signal at swin24.68 and premthakker.35. Your anonymity will be protected.

item.title: On Juneteenth, 7 Ways Trump Attacked Black History in the U.S.
item.description: Juneteenth is a significant day on the U.S. calendar that commemorates the end of slavery in 1865 . It’s long been a day celebrated by Black Americans, but it only became a federal holiday in 2021, under Joe Biden. During his first term, Donald Trump ridiculously claimed he made the holiday –…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Juneteenth is a significant day on the U.S. calendar that commemorates the end of slavery in 1865. It’s long been a day celebrated by Black Americans, but it only became a federal holiday in 2021, under Joe Biden.

During his first term, Donald Trump ridiculously claimed he made the holiday – which, according to him, “no one” had “ever heard of” – “very famous” after backlash over a rally he had planned on the day in Tulsa, Oklahoma. Last year, he didn’t recognize it at all. Instead, he complained that the country had “too many non-working holidays.”

Even if he wanted to, Trump cannot cancel Juneteenth without Congress. But that hasn’t stopped him and his administration from attempting to erase what the important day stands for and what it’s supposed to remember. Here are seven ways Trump and his administration have attacked Black history, Black Americans, and civil rights since returning to office:

Trump removed Juneteenth and Martin Luther King Jr. Day from the National Park Service’s annual free-entry days calendar, replacing them with Flag Day and, wait for it, his own birthday.

Just weeks into his second term as president, Trump’s war on diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) led to a pause on all federal activities and events related to “special observances,” including Juneteenth, Martin Luther King Jr. Day, and Black History Month, among many others.

Nearly 400 books were removed from the shelves of the U.S. Naval Academy as part of Trump’s attacks on DEI. Nearly half of the books centered around race, while many others included race as a secondary theme.

In August 2025, Trump ranted on his social media site about the country’s museums, calling them “the last remaining segment of ‘WOKE.’” The president claimed, “Everything discussed is how horrible our Country is, how bad Slavery was, and how unaccomplished the downtrodden have been — Nothing about Success.”

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has been accused of disproportionately targeting Black and female service members. Almost 60% of the senior officers Hegseth and Trump have fired are Black or women, according to Democratic Senator Jack Reed, the ranking member of the Armed Services Committee.

Those sacked included General Charles Q. Brown, who was only the second Black American to be named chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. Hegseth had previously said Brown should be fired for being “woke.”
Earlier this month, Hegseth blocked the promotion of nine Navy officers, including two Black officers. It followed a similar action in March.

The Trump administration removed a directive explicitly preventing federal contractors from having segregated facilities.

While businesses must still follow federal and state laws outlawing segregation and discrimination, experts and civil rights groups said the move “sends a clear message that the federal government does not care” if companies introduce segregation into their workplaces.

Federal employees last year dismantled an exhibit about nine people enslaved by President George Washington at the President’s House in Philadelphia. Some of the exhibits have since been restored. This month, a federal judge has ordered the reinstallation of signs and exhibits on topics like slavery and climate change nationwide.

***

It didn’t take long for Trump’s racism to influence government policy. His attacks on the country’s Black history serve as a reminder that racism in the U.S. is not a thing of the past.

Zeteo’s Mohammed Abdelbary contributed to this piece.

item.title: Israel’s Largest Weapons Contractor Could Score Huge U.S. Army Weapons Deal
item.description: Israel’s largest weapons contractor, Elbit Systems, has reportedly helped carry out its genocide in Gaza, consequently attracting protests around the world. And now, per a source familiar with the matter, the company is a top contender for a massive U.S. army contract. The U.S. subsidiary of Elbit…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Israel’s largest weapons contractor, Elbit Systems, has reportedly helped carry out its genocide in Gaza, consequently attracting protests around the world. And now, per a source familiar with the matter, the company is a top contender for a massive U.S. army contract.

The U.S. subsidiary of Elbit is collaborating with the AI drone company Anduril as it appeals to Donald Trump’s Pentagon for the contract. That relationship could prove fruitful, as Trump’s own son and his son-in-law’s family each have an interest in the drone company.

Elbit America and Anduril are competing together for a U.S. army contract seeking producers for its long-awaited program to modernize its fleet of self-propelled howitzers (tank-like vehicles used for long-range fire). Anduril has said it will incorporate its AI-driven software platform into the team’s proposed system. The army aims to award the contract by July; the army hasn’t announced a full timeline yet, but development and production for a fleet of howitzers would last years.

It would be significant for Elbit to secure the howitzer contract, former State Department official Josh Paul told Zeteo, saying there is hardly precedent for the U.S. sourcing weapons from an Israeli outfit at such a scale. “I can’t think of another Israeli co-produced system that is by itself a major defense acquisition program,” he said, adding that the Army “would be dependent on the system for a generation to come.”

The contract’s awarding period comes as Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Republicans are currently seeking to drastically fuse the U.S. and Israeli militaries by having the two countries share data, co-produce weapons, and jointly integrate AI and cyber technologies – all despite growing public calls to cut U.S. aid to Israel. Meanwhile, top U.S. intelligence agencies have warned that Israel has intensified its spying on U.S. military and government personnel.

While the Army has not announced a final contract value, it could be worth billions of dollars. That would come through development of early prototypes and testing, and then full production for an entire fleet of howitzers. To date, Elbit’s largest publicly known contracts with the U.S. have topped at around $500 million.

The Army had been considering modernization of its howitzer fleet for decades, with each past effort being canceled or phased out. It has now settled on a mission to take existing technologies, and retrofit it for U.S. Army needs – instead of trying to craft a completely new re-design, as it sought to in the past.

Elbit America operates under a Special Security Agreement with the U.S. – which, as the company itself states, is meant to mitigate “the potential impact of foreign ownership, control, or influence, and allows Elbit Systems of America and its subsidiaries to perform contracts on an equal footing with other U.S.-companies.”

Such agreements are meant to allow U.S. firms under foreign control or influence to work on defense contracts. The Government Accountability Office has repeatedly found processes like these agreements to be insufficient in providing oversight over foreign influence.

Indeed, it’s unclear how such an agreement could meaningfully mitigate foreign influence in Elbit’s case.

Elbit Systems has produced up to 85 percent of the Israeli military’s land-based equipment and drones. At least seven executive officers and one sitting board director of Elbit Systems have served in the Israeli military or the Israeli Ministry of Defense. Moreover, the Israeli-owned Elbit’s top officials serve on the board of directors of Elbit America, too: executive vice president Kobi Kagan, and CEO Bezhalel Machlis. This means a quarter of Elbit America’s board of directors has served in the Israeli military.

Elbit’s technology has been used heavily as part of Israel’s genocidal violence in Palestine and Lebanon.

Israeli forces have reportedly used Elbit aircraft to track virtually all of Gaza from the air, and identify targets and direct fire in both Gaza and Lebanon. In 2024, Israeli forces reportedly used Elbit drones in the infamous strike on a World Central Kitchen aid convoy in Gaza, which killed seven aid workers, including a Canadian-American. An Elbit company’s weapons were found to have been used in the killing of a family in Gaza, including six children. Amnesty International has identified Elbit as being among the companies “contributing to Israel’s unlawful occupation, genocide, or other crimes under international law.”

Furthermore, new reports reveal that components from Elbit America were shipped to Israel to be used in Israel’s genocide in Gaza – including for the types of weapons that have been confirmed to have been used to kill civilians in Gaza.

Domestically, in the U.S., Elbit America helps conduct surveillance, including as part of a contract to build AI-powered surveillance towers along the southern border.

All that and more has made Elbit a hot target for protesters and activists, as facilities across the world have attracted recurring demonstrations.

Among the more visible acts of protest involved activists blocking the road leading to a New Hampshire Elbit facility, throwing red paint on the building’s facade, breaking several windows, and setting off smoke flares in the colors of the Palestinian flag. The demonstrators faced extreme felony charges that carried sentences for up to 37 years in prison; they were eventually sentenced to 60 days in jail.

This week, four activists with Palestine Action were sentenced to years in prison for breaking into an Elbit Systems factory in the UK, causing over $1.5 million in damage; one of the activists was convicted of grievous bodily harm after fracturing a police officer’s spine with a sledgehammer.

European dockworkers have also interrupted shipments and refused to load arms linked to Elbit.

Elbit is not alone in seeking the coveted howitzer modernization contract, competing against several other companies vying for the award. In its bid, Elbit is collaborating with Anduril Industries, the company founded by self-proclaimed “radical Zionist” Palmer Luckey.

Anduril has deep ties to the Trump family. Donald Trump Jr.’s investment firm, 1789 Capital, has a major stake in the company. Joshua Kushner, the brother of Jared Kushner (Donald Trump’s son-in-law) also holds a major stake. Just weeks ago, it was reported that Joshua Kushner’s firm, Thrive Capital, helped co-lead a $5 billion funding round for Anduril.

Luckey, who’s a major Trump donor, has long been a supporter of the commander-in-chief, once boasting that as a teenager, he wrote a letter to Trump, urging him to run for president. He also took a secret visit to Israel earlier this year to meet with defense officials and Benjamin Netanyahu.

Elbit’s effort to secure potentially billions from the U.S. comes as Republicans work to further integrate the U.S. and Israeli militaries as part of the annual national defense authorization – in an attempt to sidestep calls to cut U.S. aid to Israel.

Republicans have inserted language into the NDAA, which is currently moving through Congress, to direct the U.S. and Israel to co-produce weapons, expand coordination in the realms of AI and biotechnology, and share U.S. military data with Israel, among other acts.

Meanwhile, Republicans just killed an amendment to the NDAA, led by Senator Elizabeth Warren, that would have barred the Pentagon from investing in companies tied to Trump, his Cabinet, and their families. Warren and others have raised concerns specifically about preferential Pentagon treatment toward companies linked to Trump Jr. and 1789 Capital.
If the Israeli-owned Elbit is to secure the howitzer contract, Paul says, it would be “just the leading edge of what’s to come if the current NDAA language makes it into law.”

item.title: ‘Just Do It Anyway’: Inside Trump’s Plans to Pay His Allies, Slush Fund or Not
item.description: Shortly after Donald Trump’s $1.776 billion slush fund of taxpayer money was announced by the Justice Department last month, it triggered such furious backlash that the administration was forced to at least pretend it was pausing the project, with acting Attorney General Todd Blanche claiming that…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Shortly after Donald Trump’s $1.776 billion slush fund of taxpayer money was announced by the Justice Department last month, it triggered such furious backlash that the administration was forced to at least pretend it was pausing the project, with acting Attorney General Todd Blanche claiming that his department was “not moving forward with the fund, period.”

Right around that time, three sources familiar with the situation tell Zeteo, senior Trump officials immediately got to work on alternate plans for delivering public funds to the president’s allies – even if the new “fund” didn’t technically exist on paper.

According to two of the sources, Trump himself had a succinct reply, when the topic of political or legal roadblocks to his slush fund came up in recent conversations with close advisers: “Just do it anyway.”

The president, the sources add, was referring to options he’d been briefed on by administration officials and Republican allies, who laid out how the Trump-Vance administration could still potentially make generous payments to numerous would-be fund applicants, even if the new, specific initiative stays tied up in court challenges.

The fund was created out of one of President Trump’s most brazen acts of corruption yet, in an administration already overflowing with titanic, unprecedented levels of lawlessness and cash grabs. Trump and his family business empire sued the IRS for $10 billion over a leak of the president’s tax returns that occurred during his first term in the White House. As a federal judge prepared to review whether it was kosher for Trump to sue his own administration, since the officials in it report to him, the Justice Department – which functions as an arm of the Trump White House – short-circuited that discussion by announcing a settlement.

In ending the suit, Trump and DOJ agreed to launch a nearly $1.8 billion dollar so-called “anti-weaponization” fund to pay out to those who claim they were unfairly targeted by the Biden administration and Democrats.

The DOJ later announced Blanche had signed a one-page addendum to the agreement attempting to shield Trump, his family, and their businesses from federal enforcement regarding his tax returns, a provision that could, coincidentally, save Trump $100 million and end a long-running tax dispute with the IRS.

Critics swiftly condemned the agreement as a “MAGA slush fund” to benefit Jan. 6 insurrectionists, people who aided Trump in attempted election theft and other scandals, and aggrieved Republicans and Trump cultists. Some elite Republican operatives and lawmakers started freaking out about how such a fund could further poison their party’s odds in the upcoming midterm elections. So far, a federal judge has indefinitely blocked the fund.

But this is Trumpland. And they think they can just do whatever they want, no matter how heinous.

One source describes an internal Trump administration memo they took part in crafting that outlined how Democratic lawmakers have severely limited options for trying to stop the Trump Justice Department from settling lawsuits or Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) claims brought by MAGA diehards, Jan. 6 rioters, or Trump supporters, and then paying out those settlements out of the Treasury Department’s Judgment Fund, an unlimited fund established by Congress so the government can pay settlements and judgments.

“You never needed a $1.8 billion fund,” this source says. “Different administrations make these payments all the time… You can get past $1.8 billion pretty fast. The Judgment Fund is unlimited and there’s no law saying you can’t hit that number.”

Indeed, the administration has already tapped the Judgment Fund liberally as it’s arranged to pay $2.5 billion to cancel offshore wind leases, purely because the president hates windmills.

After Zeteo contacted the Justice Department for comment, a DOJ official responded: “Tools to provide restitution to victims of government weaponization have always been in place across both Democrat and Republican administrations. The fact that the media is now just realizing this does not mean it’s some new, novel campaign within the department.”

One Trump administration official compares the potential “workarounds” to Trump’s $1.776 billion slush fund to how the Biden administration managed to still give out billions of dollars in student debt relief, after the initial and far more ambitious program was struck down by the U.S. Supreme Court’s conservative majority.

And as The Atlantic noted earlier this month: “Behind the scenes, Justice Department and other Trump administration officials have quietly assured allies that plans for some form of payout remain on track.”

Even as he’s privately briefed on fallback options, publicly, the president won’t even commit to shelving the new fund itself. When asked early this month if his $1.776 billion fund was still happening, Trump told reporters at the White House: “I’d have to ask the lawyers. I don’t know.” He added that the weaponization fund “was a beautiful thing” and “so important.”

Indeed, the Trump administration views rewarding the president’s disgraced comrades as such a priority that even when top appointees claim the fund has been killed, they still emphatically signal to MAGAworld: You can still get your money, guys.

“At my level, the ‌fund is dead,” Stanley Woodward, a senior DOJ official, told Reuters. “If somebody wants to submit a claim against the government and sue us, they can still do that.”

But whatever form it takes, Trump’s nearly $2 billion dollar slush fund, along with the administration’s plans to keep it going, are emblematic of the rampant lawlessness and authoritarian corruption of the second Trump presidency.

In his attempts to personally raid billions in taxpayer dollars, Trump and his team created a potential cash grab for his buddies and even some of his most violent, criminally investigated fans.

When lawmakers and the courts started telling him no, his and much of his party’s response to that was: Just do it. Fitting, given how the Trump White House operates primarily on the overarching legal principle of “what are you gonna do about it?” according to one right-wing lawyer who helped Team Trump plot out part of their second-term blitz.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 🍒 Iran Has Trump 'By the Balls,' Say Senior U.S. Officials
item.description: Editor’s Note: There will be no ‘First Draft’ tomorrow, June 19, due to the Juneteenth holiday. Enjoy the day and the long weekend, and we’ll see you again Monday morning with this newsletter. - Mehdi Happy Thursday! It’s Swin again. Virtually everybody knows America lost another war . The U.S.…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Editor’s Note: There will be no ‘First Draft’ tomorrow, June 19, due to the Juneteenth holiday. Enjoy the day and the long weekend, and we’ll see you again Monday morning with this newsletter. - Mehdi

Happy Thursday! It’s Swin again. Virtually everybody knows America lost another war. The U.S. government’s propaganda machine is kicking into overdrive to try to convince you that, No, President Trump won it, very strongly, actually. But to disprove it, you don’t need to consult your own lying eyes, to ask Trump’s liberal critics, or even to ask

That’s what I did.

So, in today’s ‘First Draft,’ we get into why everyone is more ready than ever before to call Donald Trump’s bluffs – particularly when it comes to the illegal bloodbath he started in the Middle East. Plus, JD Vance is advised to start watching his back – and his front – while Trump’s absurd politicking leaves U.S. intelligence in limbo.

Oh, and here’s some good news! Today’s edition of ‘First Draft’ has NO paywall, thanks to our sponsor, Ground News. 🗞️ 😎 🙏 So there’s no paywall today BUT you should still consider becoming a paid subscriber to Zeteo and supporting independent journalism!

Now let’s get to it…

One long year ago, I was talking to an appointee in Donald Trump’s new administration who, as many of Team Trump’s senior and mid-level officials are wont to do, felt like anonymously complaining to a member of the media about something blisteringly moronic the president had done.

This official, who sounded deeply annoyed by Trump’s first bombing of Iran, said something that has rung in my brain for the past 12 months: The Trump appointee called the U.S. president “ a lazy warmonger.”

To this day, I cannot think of a more fitting term to describe the policies and the global vision of the Trump-Vance era. Yes, the self-described “Peace President” is actually very good at starting wars. But he’s often unwilling to put in the hard, blood-caked effort exhibited by work-ethic genocidaires who came before him.

Does that laziness ultimately save some lives? Maybe. But it also gets a ton of people killed, as the world waits for Trump’s laziness to evolve into gratuitous imperial humiliation.

In his absurdly fascistic second term, he is now doing the most characteristically fascist thing he could do – surrendering, after starting a fight with what he thought was a much weaker nation. And like the sore-loser bully that he is, he can’t bring himself to admit he got his ass handed to him.

Last night, he even chose to sign the surrender document, sorry, memorandum of understanding, at… wait for it… the Palace of Versailles.

Earlier in the day, at the G7 summit in France, President Trump had again tried to sound tough, saying of the deal: “No, it’s not final. It’s a memorandum of understanding, and if I don’t like it, we’ll go back to shooting at them, dropping bombs on their head.”

He also said: “If they don’t behave, we’ll go back to dropping bombs right smack in the middle of their head.”

The problem? Trump has threatened mass slaughter, genocide, and ground invasions so many times, only to sheepishly pull back, that senior government officials both in Tehran and Washington now simply don’t believe he has the stomach to restart his war in any substantial way. This is true of many officials and aides I’ve spoken to lately at the Pentagon, White House, and elsewhere.

Furthermore, numerous Trump advisers and administration officials have told me the same thing within the past week and a half, which boils down to: We cannot afford to keep this war going much longer. We are weeks away from it plunging the global economy off a massive cliff. And not only is it shredding the Republican Party’s chances ahead of the 2026 midterm elections, but it’s been tearing apart this administration and could very well bring it down. We do not have the leverage that the Trump White House keeps publicly pretending that we have.

There was one quote – from one senior U.S. official – that stood out, given how briskly it got to the point of where Trump is at in negotiations with the Iranians:

“They’ve got him by the balls,” this official – who, like others, is remaining anonymous because they don’t want to be fired or jailed by Team Trump – noted. “We want out, and [the Iranians] know we don’t have all the cards.”

It’s such a shame… for Trump. No one believes the U.S. is negotiating from a position of strength, no matter how many times JD Vance says we are.

Trump lost this one, badly. He will soon have more time to focus on his administration’s true top priority: His war on the American people.

The more complex the story, the better. With Ground News you can see beyond the headlines and unlock hidden perspectives. For example, take this story on Trump saying that the U.S.-Iran deal is not final, threatening to resume bombing if Iran “doesn’t behave.”

Ground News is a news app and website that scans 50,000+ articles while offering built-in bias comparisons, fact checks, and funding transparency for every source.

For this story, we see that there is a split in the coverage with 26% left, 38% right, and 36% center . The headlines are the same, but the coverage couldn’t be more different. Ground News makes it easy to see the difference in how both sides are covering the same story.

The built-in factuality and ownership features also highlight the hidden angles behind every article. With Ground News’ Vantage Plan, information becomes your superpower. Learn more at ground.news/zeteox and get 40% off the unlimited Vantage Plan.

(This portion of the post was sponsored content.)

  • Terms read:A U.S. officialread outthe terms of the preliminary deal to end the war, including ending military operations, including in Lebanon; the U.S. raising its blockade and removing forces; Iran opening the Strait of Hormuz; a $300 billion reconstruction fund backed by Gulf states; sanctions lifted; and Iran not seeking nuclear weapons.
  • AdVance warning: At the G7 in France, Trumpsaidof the deal and his VP: “If it works out, I’m going to take the credit. If it doesn’t work out, I’m blaming JD.”
  • Going ballistic:Trump alsosaidhe didn’t mind Iran keeping ballistic missiles, because “it’s a little unfair for them not to have some.” Forbidding such missiles was a war aim. Of the U.S. “double tap” strike that killed more than 100 children on the first day of the war, Trumpshrugged: “Mistakes are made, war is nasty.”
  • Harsh truth:As for why he ended the war he started, Trumpsaid: “I didn’t want to see economic catastrophe. If you kept this going, that could’ve happened.”
  • Hezbollah celebrates:In Lebanon, leader Naim Qassemhaileda “big victory,” adding: “The goal was to bring down the Iranian regime, but the might of American tyranny was broken.”
  • Strikes continue:Israelcarried onkilling. Drone strikes in southern Lebanon killed one and injured several over the last 24 hours. More than 3,800 people have been killed by Israel in Lebanon since the start of the latest hostilities on March 2.

Last month, Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana was beaten by a primary opponent endorsed by Donald Trump. On Wednesday, Cassidy was therefore able to speak freely about the “memorandum of understanding” ending Trump’s illegal war on Iran.

“Reagan is rolling over in his grave,” Cassidy said. “Iran’s nuclear ambitions were not curbed, and they have learned that threatening the Strait of Hormuz works and will undoubtedly leverage it in the future. Now, Iran gets to build brand-new infrastructure under this deal.

“Before the war, the strait was open, Iran was being crushed by sanctions, and 13 service members were still alive. Now, 13 Americans are dead, families have paid billions at the pump, sanctions will be lifted, and the bombing has stopped.

“This is the worst foreign policy blunder in decades.”

It was blistering, and it was not alone.

Thom Tillis of North Carolina, freed to speak by looming retirement, told MS Now: “We’ve lost two F-18s, several other air frames, 13 people have died, several of our Middle East partners have been attacked, 365 people have been injured… $100 billion has been spent since the first kinetic strike.”

Among the commentariat, Fox host Mark Levin said the deal “requires serious changes if not outright abandonment.” Speaking to Fox, Ben Shapiro bemoaned “a disaster that does not achieve any of the actual signal goals.”

Like Trump, like Ted Cruz, Shapiro had a scapegoat ready.

“In my opinion, the Vice President of the United States, the chief negotiator on this particular project, has not well served the president.”

Back in Congress, a Republican who chose to remain anonymous told NewsNation: “Conservatives on the Hill are stunned that [JD] Vance would erase all of Trump’s military victories in such a terrible deal. Trump effectively won the war and at the eleventh hour Vance is negotiating his way to a loss.”

Might be time to buy some stab vests, JD – and wear them front and back.

  • DNI farce:After Trump delayed nominating Jay Clayton as director of national intelligence because he wants the Senate to pass a voter ID bill it doesn’t support, Democrats opposed to Trump’s acting DNI director, MAGA warrior Bill Pulte,lamented. “I am not sure whether Jay Clayton has been postponed or withdrawn,” Mark Warner said, complaining of the “level of chaos [and] incompetence.”
  • ICE disgrace:Deaths in immigration detention centers have doubled under Trump, Reutersfound. 404 MediasaidICE appears to be buying immigrants’ tax identifier information from a data broker, skirting a ban on obtaining such records.
  • False charge:In France at the G7, Trump took aim at Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner,sayinghe had a “swastika” tattoo – wrong, it was a “totenkopf,” since covered up – and bleating about being called a Nazi himself.
  • Prezzing concerns:Maryland Senator and prominent Trump critic Chris Van Hollensaidhe was “kicking the tires” on a presidential run.
  • Costly delay:Trump spending reviews slowed federal efforts to respond to the threat of screwworm, now present in Texas and New Mexico,Politicosaid.
  • Left hook:With Janeese Lewis-George set to become mayor of Washington, DC, observersnotedthat four major cities are now led or may be led by democratic socialists. New York (Zohran Mamdani) and Seattle (Katie Wilson) have incumbents, while Los Angeles (Nithya Raman) could join the column.
  • Defense case:Luigi Mangione, charged with murdering UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson, willasserta psychiatric defense in his state trial.

What Nazi-related item did Donald Trump tell Vanity Fair in 1990 he kept by his bed during his first marriage?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:More than 1,000 people have beenkilledby Israel since the so-called “ceasefire” of October last year, Palestinian authoritiessaid, includingtwoin Khan Younis on Wednesday. More than 73,000 Palestinians have been killed since October 2023.
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 G7 boost:After attending the summit in France, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskysaidleaders “agreed on additional strengthening of Ukraine’s air defense” against Russian missile and drone attacks.
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 Ukraine hits back:Ukraine launched one of its biggest drone attacks on Moscow, hitting an oil refinery for the second time this week,AP reported.
  • 🇧🇷 Brazil warning:President Luiz Inácio Lula da SilvatoldDonald Trump not to meddle in the forthcoming election, after the U.S. president bemoaned theconvictionof Eduardo Bolsonaro for interfering in his father Jair Bolsonaro’s trial over an attempted coup in 2022.

  • 🇺🇳

    Dire prospects:UN agenciessaidthat acute hunger is set to worsen in 13 hot spots, due to conflict, aid shortages, and climate shocks. Sudan, South Sudan, Yemen, and Palestine are the countries of greatest concern.


Donald Trump seems to think “Steve” enjoyed his G7 press conference a lot.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.
