item.title: What Ceasefire? Israel Has Now Killed Over 1,000 Palestinians in Gaza Since October
item.description: 73,000. That’s the number of (counted) Palestinians that Israel has killed in Gaza so far since the genocide began. 1,000. That’s the number of Palestinians that Israel has killed since the so-called “ceasefire” took effect eight months ago. I have always hated the use of the term “ceasefire” when…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
73,000. That’s the number of (counted) Palestinians that Israel has killed in Gaza so far since the genocide began.

1,000. That’s the number of Palestinians that Israel has killed since the so-called “ceasefire” took effect eight months ago.

I have always hated the use of the term “ceasefire” when it comes to Palestine. The Cambridge English dictionary defines the term as “an agreement, usually between two armies, to stop fighting in order to allow discussions about peace.” For clarity, Palestinians do not have an army; we are not “fighting” (Israel is committing genocide), and of course, there are no “discussions about peace” (nor prospect of it, given that Israel continues to steal land).

And before anyone misconstrues my words, let me be clear: Palestinians were the happiest to see Israel’s mass killings stop when the so-called “ceasefire” was announced in October 2025. From October 2023 to October 2025 – the height of Israel’s genocide on Gaza – Israel was killing, on average, over 100 Palestinians a day. Israeli forces were gleefully bombing nearly all of Gaza’s 36 hospitals, and more than 200 schools and universities (we saw the TikToks). Israeli soldiers viewed children, journalists, and medical personnel as legitimate targets. Israel and its U.S. backers derived sick pleasure in seeing Palestinians scramble for food, only for Israeli soldier’s to gun them down or bomb them.

So why do I hate the term “ceasefire”? Because “ceasefire” doesn’t mean the end of genocide – at least not for Israel. So let’s examine what it does mean for Israel.

First and foremost, to Israelis, a ceasefire means to keep killing Palestinian children, men, and women, to keep bombing tents, to keep striking people gathering food and water. This is why Israel has killed more than 1,000 Palestinians, including 200 children, and injured thousands of others since October 2025. In addition to the over 73,000 reported killings since October 2023 (which do not include deaths due to medical complications or lack of medical care), Israel has injured 172,000 others, with more than 43,000 suffering life-altering injuries.

In one weekend this month alone, Israel killed eight children across Gaza and injured others as they played football on a beach. The following week, Israeli bombs killed a paramedic, Mohammed Al-Habeel and his 5-year-old son, Mousa, as they filled containers with water. In a heartbreaking video, Mohammed’s daughter, Zeina, is seen pleading with her dead father, imploring him to “get up.”

Second, the ceasefire means to steal more land. Despite the promised “Phase 2” of the ceasefire, during which Israel was supposed to withdraw from nearly all of Gaza, we have instead seen Israel grab more land, with Netanyahu admitting that Israel has taken 60% of Gaza and promising to take even more.

That means Gaza’s entire population of about 2 million people is crammed into just slivers of land. In other words, that is three times the population of Washington, DC, forced to remain in an area that is smaller than DC itself. And, if Palestinians cross the so-called “yellow line” – a line that is supposed to demarcate where Israeli-controlled areas begin but is often not marked – they risk being killed.

Third, to Israel, the ceasefire means to continue to starve kids, deny medical assistance, and keep Palestinians imprisoned in an open-air prison, languishing in tents with no running water and no electricity.

Phase 2 of the ceasefire was to be marked by, among other things, reconstruction, rubble removal, and rehabilitation of infrastructure (water, electricity, sewage). Israel is supposed to allow at least 600 aid trucks a day into Gaza “ without interference” (of course, no one questions why Israel, the country committing genocide, gets to determine how much aid makes it through to Gaza in the first place). Phase 2 also called for the opening of the Rafah crossing. But, of course, Israel did little of this.

Due to Israel’s closures of and restrictions on lifeline passages for goods and supplies, Palestinians are still going hungry. Children suffer or are at risk of malnutrition due to a lack of fruits, vegetables, and protein. Palestinians still lack clean water, sanitation, and healthcare. Instead, rodents have infested Gaza, with children being bitten by rats and weasels. And while some have been able to leave Gaza for medical treatment, as of the end of last month, more than 18,500 Palestinians still required medical evacuation, as there is no way for them to be treated in Gaza; certainly not given that Israel blocks the entry of medical equipment and supplies. Fully opening Rafah is a matter of life or death, and Israel knows this.

Fourth, the ceasefire, to Israel, means continuing to abduct, imprison, rape, and torture Palestinians. That includes holding Gaza doctors, such as Hussam Abu Safiya (and 13 others), without charge – and with an Israeli court’s stamp of approval.

In short, in Israel, “ceasefire” means to continue to carry out genocide, at a slower pace. We knew this would happen, because we know that Israel will never be content until its Phase 2 is implemented – the complete erasure of all Palestinians.

As for mainstream media, “ceasefire” means ‘nothing to see here – time to move onto another story.’ When they do cover Israel’s killing, they use terms like “fragile ceasefire” or “imperfect ceasefire” or, on a good day, “ceasefire violation.” Of course, why bother explaining life in Gaza – there will be “discussions about peace!” Keep in mind, foreign journalists have still not been allowed independent access to Gaza.

Now, while the supposed ceasefire agreement between Iran and the United States includes mentions of ending military operations in Lebanon and “ensuring the territorial integrity and sovereignty of Lebanon,” we already know what Israel’s interpretation of “ceasefire” will be there too: kill more Lebanese, steal more land, and continue to destroy Lebanon while demanding “calm” from Lebanon. And the media’s interpretation will be: ‘Now we’ve moved on from this story too, like we moved on from Gaza because now there is peace.’

Diana Buttu is a Haifa-based lawyer and analyst who was a legal adviser to the Palestinian negotiating team in the early 2000s and is a frequent commentator and writer on Palestinian and Israeli issues. She is also a practitioner in residence at Georgetown University in Qatar. She writes Zeteo’s ‘A Diary from a Palestinian in Israel.’

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Donald Trump Lost the Iran War. Here's the Evidence
item.description: As Donald Trump prepares to sign off on a much-awaited and much-needed memorandum of understanding , or MOU, to end his war against Iran on Friday, a very simple truth needs to be recognized and acknowledged by each and every one of us: Donald Trump lost his war against Iran. Yes, he lost. Badly.…
item.author: Mehdi from Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
As Donald Trump prepares to sign off on a much-awaited and much-needed memorandum of understanding, or MOU, to end his war against Iran on Friday, a very simple truth needs to be recognized and acknowledged by each and every one of us: Donald Trump lost his war against Iran.

Yes, he lost. Badly.

He failed. Miserably.

He was defeated. Overwhelmingly.

Don’t take my word for it. Take Donald Trump’s. Look at what he himself said were the goals of this war from the very beginning. On Feb. 28, the same day he launched his illegal and unprovoked assault on the Islamic Republic, the president posted an eight-minute video address to his Truth Social account.

Three and a half months later, only two questions matter: What did he promise in that address? And have those promises been kept?

That’s what Trump announced. Yet, according to U.S. intelligence estimates, leaked to the New York Times last month, “Iran still fields about 70 percent of its mobile launchers across the country and has retained roughly 70 percent of its prewar missile stockpile,” including both ballistic and cruise missiles.

That’s a lot of missiles.

The 14-paragraph draft memorandum between the U.S. and Iran, as released by U.S. officials, makes no mention of Iran’s missiles.

On this point, at least, he delivered. Less than a month into the war, U.S. Central Command announced that it had destroyed 92% of Iran’s largest naval vessels, including the IRINS Makran, the crown jewel of the Iranian fleet and the country’s single largest warship – a claim later corroborated by satellite imagery. Last month, CENTCOM Commander Adm. Brad Cooper went even further, testifying before Congress that the United States had set Iran’s navy back by “a generation.”

The problem for Trump? None of that appears to have solved the key strategic maritime challenge it was supposed to address.

Despite the devastation inflicted on its fleet, Tehran has retained its ability to exert control over the Strait of Hormuz through a mix of naval mines, fast attack boats, and the ever-present threat of drone strikes. Which raises an obvious question: what is the value of “annihilating” Iran’s navy if Iran can still determine which ships enter or leave the world’s most important waterway – and do so without fear of being challenged?

As one source familiar with the latest U.S. intelligence assessments told CNN earlier this week: “We have now handed Iran de facto control over the strait – a weapon more powerful than any nuke.”

How’s that worked out? Well, just ask Israel.

Since the start of the war, Hezbollah has killed at least 30 Israeli soldiers and wounded more than 1,300. It has continued to fire rockets into northern Israel, despite Israel’s brutal bombardment of southern Lebanon.

In other words, the Iran-backed militant group still very much retains the personnel, capability, and intent to “destabilize” U.S. and Israeli interests in the region. Indeed, far from weakening the relationship between Tehran and Hezbollah, the conflict appears to have strengthened it – with Iran reportedly insisting that the MOU include a withdrawal of Israeli troops from Lebanon.

And then there is Trump, who, rather than destroying Hezbollah, recently boasted of having had “a very good call” with the militant group. (Can you imagine the howls from the right if Barack Obama or Joe Biden had done the same?)

The 14-point draft memorandum between the U.S. and Iran, as released by U.S. officials, makes no mention of Hezbollah or any other Iranian proxies in the region.

Iran, of course, was not building a nuclear weapon – as Trump’s outgoing director of national intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, testified to Congress last year. But even if we accept the Trump administration’s oft-repeated rationale for this war – that Iran possessed enough highly enriched uranium to produce a bomb within roughly a year, what nuclear experts call its “breakout time” – it is worth noting that literally nothing has changed on that front since Feb. 28.

Despite Trump and his minions selling the attack on Iran as an urgent necessity, a last-ditch effort to stop Iran from crossing a nuclear threshold, Reuters revealed last month that “US intelligence assessments indicate that the time Iran would ​need to build a nuclear weapon has not changed since last summer,” when analysts put Iran’s breakout timeline at around 12 months. Earlier this month, Bloomberg News reported on an analysis from the IAEA, the UN’s nuclear watchdog, suggesting the potential nuclear threat from Iran’s “large inventory of near-bomb-grade uranium” has gone up, not down, since the start of the war.

So, these were the four goals Trump outlined on the day the war began. Earlier that morning, in a phone interview with the Washington Post, he also included regime change as an explicit goal of his bombing campaign. “All I want is freedom for the people,” Trump told the Post. “I want a safe nation, and that’s what we’re going to have.”

Regime change? Not only has Ayatollah Khamenei been replaced as the supreme leader of Iran by Ayatollah Khamenei, but Trump has also been heaping praise on the younger Khamenei. Freedom for the people? Most Iran experts agree that the government in Tehran is now even more repressive, militarized, and hardline than it was before the conflict began.

The 14-point draft memorandum between the U.S. and Iran, as released by U.S. officials, makes no mention of regime change, freedom, or democracy in Iran.

To be clear: this war has been a complete and utter failure. Trump has clearly failed to achieve his own stated objectives. The goals he laid out at the outset remain unmet. And, perhaps most humiliatingly for the president, the war is ending in exactly the way he repeatedly promised it would not.

Back in April, in a Truth Social post, Trump declared that the United States would “get all Nuclear ‘Dust’” – his child-like term for the roughly half-ton of highly enriched uranium that Iran is believed to possess. He was equally adamant that “no money” would “exchange hands in any way, shape, or form.”

Fast forward to this week.

Speaking to reporters at the G7 summit in France on Tuesday, Trump suggested he may not even attempt to recover the highly enriched uranium, which is believed to be buried at facilities near Isfahan. “You could make the case, why even bother?” he said. “It’s not very valuable stuff.”

Not very valuable? This is the same material that the Trump administration spent months warning could form the basis of an Iranian nuclear weapon. The same material whose possession by Iran was invoked by the U.S. to justify a deadly and disastrous war.

Then there is the money. On Monday, Vice President JD Vance confirmed to CBS News that Iran “could have access” not only to their own frozen assets but also to a $300 billion reconstruction fund backed by Gulf countries. So much for Trump’s promise that not a single dollar would change hands.

But here is how we really know the president of the United States lost this war.

Less than a week after he began bombing the Islamic Republic, Trump declaimed on Truth Social: “There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER.”

And yet, on Friday in Switzerland, a U.S. delegation led by Vice President Vance is expected to sit down with the Iranians and sign – yes – a deal.

Then again, perhaps Trump wasn’t entirely wrong. Perhaps this war did end in “unconditional surrender.”

His own.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Which Crimes Could Trump and His Allies *Actually* Be Prosecuted For? Legal Expert Explains
item.description: “If we lose the midterms, and we lose 2028, some in this room are going to prison, myself included.” That was former Trump adviser Steve Bannon , speaking at a conservative event last year. But is he right? When Donald Trump eventually leaves power, will his allies walk free, or face a legal…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“If we lose the midterms, and we lose 2028, some in this room are going to prison, myself included.”

That was former Trump adviser Steve Bannon, speaking at a conservative event last year. But is he right? When Donald Trump eventually leaves power, will his allies walk free, or face a legal reckoning? And can presidential immunity shield him forever?

The answer, former federal prosecutor Joyce Vance tells Zeteo’s John Harwood, is complicated.

Watch the full video above to hear Vance break down who could realistically face prosecution for some of the Trump administration’s most serious crimes: from Pete Hegseth’s military strikes on boats in the Caribbean, to the ICE and DHS killings of U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis.

We’ve made this conversation free for all subscribers, but if you support the work we do, consider upgrading to a

This was a live conversation on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, where subscribers could ask questions. Be sure to subscribe to catch the next Substack Live.

Catch up on more from Zeteo:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: Trump Switching Back from War on Iran to War on Americans
item.description: On this day in 1972, five men were arrested for breaking into the Democratic National Committee HQ at the Watergate complex in Washington, DC. A little more than two years later, President Richard Nixon resigned over the scandal fueled by the burglary. Good morning! Andrew and Swin here. Donald…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1972, five men were arrested for breaking into the Democratic National Committee HQ at the Watergate complex in Washington, DC. A little more than two years later, President Richard Nixon resigned over the scandal fueled by the burglary.

Good morning! Andrew and Swin here. Donald Trump’s catastrophically reckless Iran “excursion” might be coming to an end, but that doesn’t mean he’s done waging war. No, his latest war of choice is unfortunately already unfolding, here in the U.S again.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump’s ICE operations are surging, and so are his garbage lawsuits against protesters; workers are reduced to pouring hydrogen peroxide into the reflecting pool as green algae covers Trump’s brilliant blue bottom; and Lindsey Graham is clutching for something good in the Iran fiasco.

As Donald Trump’s failed war in Iran allegedly nears its conclusion, as a humiliating and well-deserved defeat, our authoritarian president is pivoting back to his other unsuccessful war – his assault on Americans.

Trump never truly ended his siege of Minnesota, and he never stopped deploying violent, masked federal agents to kidnap immigrants and abuse U.S. citizens. But ever since he launched his illegal war on Iran in February, his administration has been largely consumed with waging that regional bloodbath, to the slight expense of his ability to wage war on his fellow citizens.

Nowadays – that is, when they’re not contemplating invading Cuba next – various members of the Trumpworld elite want to get back to basics, meaning: finding new ways to terrorize people in the United States.

One Trump adviser even explicitly told us that it was “a good thing” the president was seemingly trying to wind down his war in Iran, because “we need to focus on the terrorists here and the problems we have at home.”

For weeks, Trump officials have been telling us about plans to ramp up ICE operations again in publicly confrontational ways before the end of the year. While there’s unease within the administration about going as far as they did during the bloody, chaotic heights of their Minnesota occupation – a campaign that caused a severe, widespread public backlash – the sources tell us that officials in the White House and the Department of Homeland Security don’t want to be seen as going soft, by their voting base or their enemies.

Earlier this year, the Trump White House tried to quell a rebellion led by anti-immigration hardliners who accused the administration of caving too much to liberal pressure. White House officials kept privately assuring these allies that the ICE blitzes would ratchet back up to horror-show levels “after the midterms.” Evidently, the administration has been convinced that they need to deploy at least some of that before the midterms, too.

In recent weeks, the administration has been violently arresting protesters outside ICE’s Delaney Hall facility in New Jersey, where detainees have reportedly been subjected to horrid conditions and sexual abuse. Last week, Trump’s border czar, Tom Homan, said the administration is planning to “surge” ICE agents to New York, while making sure to pledge it wouldn’t look as bad as Trump’s Twin Cities invasion: “I will not let Minnesota happen.”

DHS on Monday posted one of its typically gross propaganda videos, one from Washington, DC, where the administration has started surging agents again. The video shows masked agents cutting down a banner reading: “Chinga la migra,” meaning, “fuck the immigration cops.” An agent holding the balled-up banner says, “We’re taking America back, baby.” The video goes on to show masked agents arresting numerous men who seem to pose zero threat.

The administration is also getting back to bringing ridiculous criminal cases against anti-Trump protesters.

The Trump Justice Department, which functions as an arm of the White House, unveiled a conspiracy case on Tuesday against members of “a Minneapolis-based direct action group with antifa ties,” as the DOJ put it. (For context, Trump’s administration has adopted a national-security strategy that treats antifascists and people with common left-wing views, more broadly, as if they have some sort of nexus to terrorism that merits investigation and enforcement.)

The case is eerily reminiscent of the DOJ prosecution of the “Broadview Six,” which included former Zeteo contributor Kat Abughazaleh, for allegedly blocking vehicles at an ICE facility outside Chicago. The DOJ was forced to dismiss all charges in that case once the judge reviewed grand jury transcripts and found evidence of extensive alleged prosecutorial misconduct.

As in the Broadview Six case, the Minnesota activists have been charged, first and foremost, with “conspiracy to impede or injure a federal officer.”

Reacting to the Minnesota case, Abughazaleh posted: “As the government raids ‘antifa groups’ in Minneapolis with the SAME charges levied against myself and the rest of the Broadview Six, we need to be asking how they got this indictment. And as charges (hopefully) get dropped, we must remember the process is the punishment.”

By Tuesday afternoon, federal agents were liberally pepper-spraying protesters outside the Minneapolis courthouse.

Earlier this year, sources with direct knowledge of the matter tell us, officials in the Trump Justice Department and elsewhere in the federal apparatus were determined to bring sweeping criminal charges against peaceful protesters and activist leaders in Minnesota. So far, they’d brought some cases against anti-ICE activists, but not to the degree that certain senior Trump officials, such as White House policy architect Stephen Miller, craved.

Administration personnel examined everything from the way Minnesota activists and demonstrators were organizing on encrypted messaging apps, to how they used whistles (whistles!) to warn fellow residents of the presence of the armed, masked federal officers.

Even with the deranged standards of the second Trump administration, there was a significant degree of internal pushback to criminally prosecuting protesters over things like their use of whistles. “It’s a tough sell,” one administration official conceded to us. “It’s hard making people afraid of people with whistles.”

Be that as it may, these new charges against anti-Trump and anti-DHS protesters in Minnesota don’t sound much better.

As Abughazaleh’s partner, The Onion CEO Ben Collins, wrote Tuesday, “These people are going to walk either way, but they should ask for grand jury transcripts immediately.”

  • Deal details:Bloombergpublishedwhat it said was the 14-point draft memorandum of understanding between the U.S. and Iran, which, the news outletwrote, gives Iran “the right to sell oil immediately, tap a $300 billion development fund and get eventual access to its frozen assets.” Reuters earlierreportedthat more than half of the $300 billion private development fund had been committed.
  • Lacking resolution:In the U.S. Senate, Republicansblockeda war powers measure meant to assert congressional authority over Iran policy. It was alsoreportedthat a little-noticed requirement for the administration to tell Congress about Iranian drone attacks could scupper key parts of Trump’s deal. Trumpsaidhe would send the deal to Congress, but only after a formal signing on Friday.
  • Trump’s warning:“It’s a memorandum of understanding and if I don’t like it, we’ll go back to shooting at them, dropping bombs,” the U.S. president said today.
  • Lebanon talks:As Israeli strikescontinuedin southern Lebanon, Iran’s top diplomatsaidthe deal requires Israel to withdraw from the country. U.S. officials have said that isn’t so, while Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said forces would stay “as long as necessary.” Hezbollahsaidit was promised Iran would not sign a nuclear deal with the U.S., unless Israel withdraws.
  • ‘All fronts’:On Lebanon, the draft agreement published by Bloomberg states the MOU establishes an “immediate and permanent end to the war on all fronts, including Lebanon.”
  • War crimes:AmnestysaidIsrael’s use of forced displacement orders in southern Lebanon constituted a war crime. More than 1 million people have been displaced since the latest escalation began.

“And then I see the disinfectant, where it knocks it out in a minute. One minute. And is there a way we can do something like that, by injection inside or almost a cleaning.”

So said Donald Trump on April 23, 2020, talking about maybe using bleach to kill COVID-19 inside human beings. More than six years later, it’s not impossible to imagine Trump saying something similar to crews working on his controversial renovations of Washington, DC.

On Tuesday, workers were seen pouring “12% hydrogen peroxide” into the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, the bottom of which Trump had painted “American flag blue,” only for vivid green algae to bloom. Aerial shots suggested the treatment might be working, up to a point. At lunchtime, the edges of the pool were blue, the center green. That wasn’t surprising: this is DC in summer, when the sun beats down and inconvenient green things sprout in any spot they can find.

If it’s an embarrassment for Trump, it’s an increasingly costly one, too. As widely reported, pool work has now cost $16 million. The vast majority went to the firm that painted the pool, but $1.7 million went to Green Water Solutions, which specializes in beating algae. On Monday, the Trump administration said GWS had “successfully destroyed the algae bloom that has plagued every pool reopening since 1922.” The next day, workers poured their chemicals.

As it happens, Trump’s famous 2020 remarks about unorthodox COVID treatments also offer a suggestion of what he might try next in 2026: light.

“So supposing we hit the body [of water] with a tremendous – whether it’s ultraviolet or just very powerful light… and then I said, ‘Supposing you brought the light inside the body [of water], which you can do either through the [surface] or in some other way… It sounds interesting.”

It sounds ridiculous. So does Trump’s reflecting pool farce. Might as well give it a go.

  • Trump move:Trumpsaidtoday he would delay his nomination of Jay Clayton as the director of national intelligence, in an effort to pressure Congress to take action on a voting restrictions bill, which currently doesn’t have enough support to pass. Clayton’s confirmation hearing was set for later today.
  • Ballroom bull:TheWashington Postobtaineda contractor’s estimate showing the cost for Donald Trump’s White House ballroom at $600 million, more than half from taxpayers, before Trump put the cost at $400 million, paid by donors.
  • First aide:TheWall Street Journalcited seven sourcessayingBoris Epshteyn, a lawyer and adviserknownas Trump’s “psychiatrist,” helped Indian billionaire Gautam Adani beat a DOJ fraud case. Epshteyn and Adani denied it.
  • Kash cash:Rep. Jamie Raskinwroteto Kash Patel about “troubling reports” the FBI director “may be using part of the budget… as a personal slush fund” to pay “loyalist MAGA henchmen,” to the extent checks have started to bounce.
  • Georgia votes:Trump-backed Rep. Mike Collinswonthe GOP Senate run-off, setting up a battle with incumbent Democrat Jon Ossoff. In state Senate primaries,Uncommittedactivist Ruwa Rommanwonin district seven.
  • Plot alleged:Five people face federal charges over anallegedplan to attack Trump’s White House UFC event last Sunday with drones and guns. The DOJcitedthe charges in a new court filing pleading to let his ballroom move forward.
  • New low:ICE loosened standards for the treatment of detainees at the request of a contractor facing lawsuits over facilities it manages, thePostscooped.
  • Tipping the scales:Trump’s DOJintervenedon behalf of Elon Musk’s xAI against the NAACP, which says the company’s gas turbines endanger public health. The military apparently needs Grok Gov for national security; the Twitter version of Grok once spent a day calling itself“MechaHitler.”
  • Ground stop:Trump’s July 4 rally and huge fireworks display willcausetwo days of disruptions at DC Reagan Airport in one of its busiest spells of the year.

Trump lawyer Boris Epshteyn has twice been arrested, over a bar fight in 2014 and an accusation of groping two women in a club in 2021. In which city did both arrests occur?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel killed two brothers near Nuseirat refugee camp, officialssaid, bringing the death toll since the so-called “ceasefire” to the brink of 1,000. In Jerusalem, the Israeli Supreme Courtrejectedan appeal to release Hussam Abu Safiya, a Gaza doctor held without charge for more than a year.
  • 🇮🇱 Court press:Benjamin Netanyahuclaimedprosecutors in his corruption trial acted like the “Stasi,” the East German secret police. In an angry outburst, the Israeli prime minister fumed: “What we have here is a political persecution like a police state!”
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 Sanctions move:At the G7, Trumpsaidsanctions on Russia over its invasion of Ukraine, eased during his war on Iran, could be put back in place “soon” because “the oil is now flowing.” In the English Channel, a Russian navy shipfiredwarning shots near a British-registered pleasure yacht.

  • 🇭🇹

    Haiti visit:UN Secretary General António GuttieresvisitedHaiti amid a surge in gang violence. Prime Minister Alix Didier-Fils-Aimé said he “had a frank conversation about what’s happening and the vision the government has for the future.” Haiti has had no president since July 2021, when Jovenel Moïse was killed.


Senator Lindsey Graham thinks Donald Trump “achieved” something with his illegal war on Iran. Clue: it’s not something hawks like Graham wanted, like regime change, but it is something that existed before Trump started the war. In short… it’s ridiculous. Business as usual for Senator ‘Stinkball,’ then.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Asawin Suebsaeng contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

That wasn’t surprising: this is DC in summer, when the sun beats down and inconvenient green things sprout in any spot they can find.

If it’s an embarrassment for Trump, it’s an increasingly costly one, too. As widely reported, pool work has now cost $16 million. The vast majority went to the firm that painted the pool, but $1.7 million went to Green Water Solutions, which specializes in beating algae. On Monday, the Trump administration said GWS had “successfully destroyed the algae bloom that has plagued every pool reopening since 1922.” The next day, workers poured their chemicals.

As it happens, Trump’s famous 2020 remarks about unorthodox COVID treatments also offer a suggestion of what he might try next in 2026: light.

“So supposing we hit the body [of water] with a tremendous – whether it’s ultraviolet or just very powerful light… and then I said, ‘Supposing you brought the light inside the body [of water], which you can do either through the [surface] or in some other way… It sounds interesting.”

It sounds ridiculous. So does Trump’s reflecting pool farce. Might as well give it a go.

  • Trump move:Trumpsaidtoday he would delay his nomination of Jay Clayton as the director of national intelligence, in an effort to pressure Congress to take action on a voting restrictions bill, which currently doesn’t have enough support to pass. Clayton’s confirmation hearing was set for later today.
  • Ballroom bull:TheWashington Postobtaineda contractor’s estimate showing the cost for Donald Trump’s White House ballroom at $600 million, more than half from taxpayers, before Trump put the cost at $400 million, paid by donors.
  • First aide:TheWall Street Journalcited seven sourcessayingBoris Epshteyn, a lawyer and adviserknownas Trump’s “psychiatrist,” helped Indian billionaire Gautam Adani beat a DOJ fraud case. Epshteyn and Adani denied it.
  • Kash cash:Rep. Jamie Raskinwroteto Kash Patel about “troubling reports” the FBI director “may be using part of the budget… as a personal slush fund” to pay “loyalist MAGA henchmen,” to the extent checks have started to bounce.
  • Georgia votes:Trump-backed Rep. Mike Collinswonthe GOP Senate run-off, setting up a battle with incumbent Democrat Jon Ossoff. In state Senate primaries,Uncommittedactivist Ruwa Rommanwonin district seven.
  • Plot alleged:Five people face federal charges over anallegedplan to attack Trump’s White House UFC event last Sunday with drones and guns. The DOJcitedthe charges in a new court filing pleading to let his ballroom move forward.
  • New low:ICE loosened standards for the treatment of detainees at the request of a contractor facing lawsuits over facilities it manages, thePostscooped.
  • Tipping the scales:Trump’s DOJintervenedon behalf of Elon Musk’s xAI against the NAACP, which says the company’s gas turbines endanger public health. The military apparently needs Grok Gov for national security; the Twitter version of Grok once spent a day calling itself“MechaHitler.”
  • Ground stop:Trump’s July 4 rally and huge fireworks display willcausetwo days of disruptions at DC Reagan Airport in one of its busiest spells of the year.

Trump lawyer Boris Epshteyn has twice been arrested, over a bar fight in 2014 and an accusation of groping two women in a club in 2021. In which city did both arrests occur?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel killed two brothers near Nuseirat refugee camp, officialssaid, bringing the death toll since the so-called “ceasefire” to the brink of 1,000. In Jerusalem, the Israeli Supreme Courtrejectedan appeal to release Hussam Abu Safiya, a Gaza doctor held without charge for more than a year.
  • 🇮🇱 Court press:Benjamin Netanyahuclaimedprosecutors in his corruption trial acted like the “Stasi,” the East German secret police. In an angry outburst, the Israeli prime minister fumed: “What we have here is a political persecution like a police state!”
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 Sanctions move:At the G7, Trumpsaidsanctions on Russia over its invasion of Ukraine, eased during his war on Iran, could be put back in place “soon” because “the oil is now flowing.” In the English Channel, a Russian navy shipfiredwarning shots near a British-registered pleasure yacht.

  • 🇭🇹

    Haiti visit:UN Secretary General António GuttieresvisitedHaiti amid a surge in gang violence. Prime Minister Alix Didier-Fils-Aimé said he “had a frank conversation about what’s happening and the vision the government has for the future.” Haiti has had no president since July 2021, when Jovenel Moïse was killed.


Senator Lindsey Graham thinks Donald Trump “achieved” something with his illegal war on Iran. Clue: it’s not something hawks like Graham wanted, like regime change, but it is something that existed before Trump started the war. In short… it’s ridiculous. Business as usual for Senator ‘Stinkball,’ then.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Asawin Suebsaeng contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Susan Collins’s Husband Worked for the Lobbyist Running Her Super PAC
item.description: Senator Susan Collins’s husband has worked for the lobbyist leading the primary super PAC supporting her re-election campaign in Maine, and he apparently worked for the lobbyist again last year, documents reviewed by Zeteo show. According to Collins’s most recent financial disclosure, her husband,…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Senator Susan Collins’s husband has worked for the lobbyist leading the primary super PAC supporting her re-election campaign in Maine, and he apparently worked for the lobbyist again last year, documents reviewed by Zeteo show.

According to Collins’s most recent financial disclosure, her husband, Tom Daffron, consulted last year for Chesapeake Enterprises, which appears to be the lobbying firm based in Dallas, Texas. According to the form, Daffron was paid more than $1,000. Exact figures do not have to be disclosed.

The nature of Daffron’s work for Chesapeake Enterprises is not clear. A firm by that name is run by Scott Reed, a lobbyist and former strategist for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce. Reed is one of Collins’s most important backers: He chairs Pine Tree Results PAC, a pro-Collins super PAC that spent $4 million on negative ads against Democrat Graham Platner even before primary day in Maine. (Pine Tree Results was formed on Jan. 3, 2025.)

Daffron and Reed, who led a pro-Collins outside group in 2020, worked together as lobbyists for Chesapeake Enterprises more than two decades ago, with Daffron serving as an executive vice president at the firm from 2001 to 2004.

Collins’s 2025 financial disclosure lists her husband as working last year for Chesapeake Enterprises LLC in Dallas, Texas. State records do not show such an LLC registered in Texas. They do, however, list a Chesapeake Enterprises Inc. in Dallas, with Reed named as its chairman and director.

Super PACs are legally barred from coordinating with the campaigns they support. If Collins’s husband has been working for the man leading her super PAC, as appears to be the case, it would not necessarily represent any kind of prohibited conduct. The situation is, however, highly unusual.

Brendan Fischer, director strategic investigations at the Campaign Legal Center, says this case “is yet another example of the porous boundaries between campaigns and purportedly independent super PACs.”

Spokespeople for Collins’s Senate office and campaign did not respond to Zeteo’s requests for comment, nor did Reed.

Collins, 73, was first elected to the U.S. Senate in 1996. Initially promising to serve only two terms, she is now seeking a sixth. She married Daffron, 14 years her senior, in 2012. As described in a New York Times wedding announcement, he was then chief operating officer of Jefferson Consulting Group, a lobbying and consulting firm in Washington, DC.

Before that, Daffron was chief of staff to Republican Senators William Cohen (Maine), Fred Thompson (Tennessee), and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska), and managed the 2000 presidential campaign of Elizabeth Dole, Bob Dole’s wife and a former secretary of transportation and labor.

Reed was campaign manager for Senator Bob Dole, the GOP presidential candidate who was beaten by Bill Clinton in 1996.

In the run-up to the Maine primary, Platner, a U.S. Marine turned oysterman and populist progressive, was the subject of a string of negative stories in the mainstream media. Nonetheless he cruised to victory, becoming the Democratic nominee for U.S. Senate. In his victory speech, he took aim at Collins’s links to the DC influence industry – and those of her husband.

“Susan Collins has used her privilege and power to funnel $60 million in federal contracts, $60 million of our tax dollars, to her lobbyist husband,” Platner charged. “If that’s not corruption, I don’t know what is.”

Collins denied that claim last week, telling a local news station, “It’s just not true, and it’s obvious that Mr. Platner has a problem with the truth.” However, Platner did not pull this figure out of thin air: Salon previously reported that Daffron’s firm, Jefferson Consulting, “took in nearly $60 million in federal contracts during his time there.”

“Susan Collins has gotten 21 times wealthier just in the last 15 years,” Platner continued as part of his victory speech, asking: “Has anybody else gotten 21 times wealthier since Susan Collins was elected to office? Does Maine have 21 times the schools and hospitals? No, we have less. Susan Collins is getting rich while we’re getting screwed.”

Platner also said he would be “a senator for the people who cannot afford to buy a senator,” and said he would “serve you, the people of Maine, not some lobbyist or party boss.”

Further examination of lobbying filings by Chesapeake Enterprises reveals its clients include a company called Chupacabra Ventures, which engaged the lobbying firm to work on “healthcare issues.”

According to Texas corporate records, Chupacabra Ventures lists its general partner as Chupacabra GP, LLC, which lists its sole member as Carlos Zaffirini, who runs Adelanto HealthCare Ventures (AHCV). A healthcare consulting company that works with safety-net hospitals and healthcare providers, AHCV focuses on Medicaid reimbursements.

Collins has taken credit for proposing and negotiating the Rural Health Transformation Program, which set up a $50 billion rural hospital relief fund, meant to partially counteract the massive Medicaid cuts contained in Donald Trump’s “Big Beautiful Bill,” the spending package rammed through Congress last year. The law is expected to kick millions of people off Medicaid, the government health insurance program for low-income and disabled Americans, through onerous work requirements.

Collins cast an important vote to advance the Big Beautiful Bill to the Senate floor before voting against it on final passage. The law did include the Collins priority, a rural hospital fund theoretically meant to help healthcare providers deal with the wave of Medicaid cuts.

Publicly available documentation shows that AHCV closely monitored Medicaid changes under the One Big Beautiful Bill, including plans for the Rural Health Transformation Program.

Zaffirini, who has a history of donating to both parties, contributed the maximum to Collins this cycle. He did not respond to Zeteo’s request for comment.

item.title: Bari Weiss’s Newest Hire Has a Long History of Anti-Muslim Remarks
item.description: Hello, and welcome to this week’s early edition of ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In today’s edition, we dig into Bari Weiss’s latest big hire at CBS News – and the allegations of Islamophobia…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Hello, and welcome to this week’s early edition of ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In today’s edition, we dig into Bari Weiss’s latest big hire at CBS News – and the allegations of Islamophobia that have followed him throughout his UK broadcasting and political career. Also, is woke algae ruining Trump’s big, beautiful reflecting pool? And how are the MAGA hawks at Fox dealing with Trump’s supposed Iran peace deal?

“No, don’t try that one on me!” Sky News presenter Trevor Phillips dismissively sneered at UK Green Party leader Zack Polanski last month during an extremely contentious interview.

That contemptuous response came after Polanski, the only Jewish party leader in the UK, as well as the only leader who is outspoken against the genocide in Gaza, wondered why his “Jewish identity [was] being erased from this conversation” as Phillips continuously twisted the left-wing politician’s previous remarks about the threat of antisemitism in the UK.

Polanski noted that many Jews – such as himself – do not feel that pro-Palestinian rallies should be perceived as unsafe hate marches, while acknowledging that attacks on Jewish sites and people in Britain are on the rise. But Phillips chose to ignore and even belittle Polanski’s personal experiences and feelings on the matter. Even when the progressive leader brought up his own recent brushes with antisemitism.

Five weeks after Phillips “monstered” Polanski on air, as Zeteo’s own Owen Jones observed in a recent column in The Guardian, CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss announced that the longtime British broadcaster and political figure had been hired as her network’s senior global affairs correspondent.

“Trevor Phillips cuts through the noise. His decades-long career is a masterclass in seeing beyond groupthink and pursuing the truth,” Weiss said in a statement about his hiring, noting that his reporting will appear on all of CBS News’s programming. “Trevor’s deep knowledge of geopolitics and history will be an incredible asset at CBS News, where he’ll quickly become an indispensable voice for audiences across all platforms.”

Following the five-alarm fire she set at the network in recent weeks with her “Black Thursday” firings at ‘60 Minutes,’ which has sparked growing murmurs that Weiss could be demoted ahead of the CNN-CBS News merger, one wonders why her next big move was to hire a 72-year-old opinion host unfamiliar to American viewers. (At the very least, it doesn’t seem like this will move the needle with younger audiences, which Weiss’s digital-first strategy was supposed to appeal to.)

“I guess I’d be looking at what correspondent work he had done before. He is a presenter. That’s different,” one former CBS foreign bureau staffer told me.

Of course, with Weiss – a former New York Times opinion columnist and founder of the anti-woke blog The Free Press – seeing herself as a heterodox thought leader, it doesn’t take much digging to see exactly why Phillips appeals to her.

First, Phillips will be reporting out of the network’s London bureau, which has already seen a huge shakeup after Weiss pushed out highly respected bureau chief Claire Day after a freelance cameraman accused Day of running the bureau like a “Hamas cell.” She replaced Day with Shayndi Raice, a former Wall Street Journal editor with no broadcast experience but similar views on Israel and Gaza as the “Zionist fanatic” Weiss.

And while Phillips comes to CBS News following a long and distinguished career in British politics, writing and broadcasting, it is not without a fair bit of controversy. He was a Labour Party politician who served as the chairman of the UK’s Equality and Human Rights Commission (and the Commission for Racial Equality, its predecessor) for nearly a decade, leaving in September 2012.

His management of the EHRC was wracked with turmoil, which saw six of the organization’s commissioners leave due to this “divisive leadership” that had led to “deep discontentment and dissatisfaction. It was also during his time in charge of the race commission that Phillips, in 2004, said that the UK should stop supporting multiculturalism and instead “assert a core of Britishness.”

His views on Islam, however, are what have drawn the most notice in recent years. Having initially helped usher in the term “Islamophobia” due to his work on a groundbreaking 1997 report by the Runnymede Trust about rampant anti-Muslim sentiment in the UK, by January 2016, Phillips was insisting that British Muslims “see the world differently from the rest of us.” In an April 2016 documentary, he argued that while anti-Muslim sentiment was well-documented, the 1997 commission “got almost everything else wrong” in its report.

Warning that Muslims were building “nations within nations” in the West, Phillips said that the UK was “heading in entirely the wrong direction” and there was” a life-and-death struggle for the soul of British Islam” in which the population needed to “take sides.” Right-wing author and notorious Islamophobe Douglas Murray, who recently joined Weiss’s The Free Press as a weekly columnist, heaped praise on Phillips at the time for breaking “taboos.”

A month later, Phillips published a 100-page paper in which he argued that Britain was “sleepwalking to catastrophe” over race, drawing a striking parallel to the late British Conservative politician Enoch Powell’s infamous ‘Rivers of Blood’ speech.

“Rome may not yet be in flames, but I think I can smell the smouldering whilst we hum to the music of liberal self-delusion,” Phillips wrote, adding: “Any attempt to ask whether aspects of minority disadvantage may be self-inflicted is denounced as ‘blaming the victim.’ Instead, we prefer to answer any difficult questions by focusing on the historic prejudices of the dominant majority. In short, it’s all about white racism.”

In 2017, Phillips said placing a Christian child with Muslim foster parents was “akin to child abuse.” He later claimed, according to an internal Labour Party investigation, that few British Muslims wore poppies to remember the UK’s war dead on Remembrance Sunday.

All of this eventually led the Labour Party to suspend Phillips over his allegedly Islamophobic views and comments in March 2020.

“They say I am accusing Muslims of being different. Well, actually, that’s true. The point is Muslims are different. And in many ways, I think that’s admirable,” Phillips declared following the suspension. Phillips, who had blamed the suspension on “political gangsterism” led by then-party leader Jeremy Corbyn, was then quietly reinstated just over a year later under the new centrist leadership of Keir Starmer.

Since then, Phillips has remained outspoken in his criticism of the Muslim community in Britain. After his tense back-and-forth with Polanski, he told Times Radio that there’s “an Islamist community that wants to kill Jews and they want to use the arguments that are being had about the Middle East as cover for their malign intent,” adding that the Green Party “draws its support from that community.”

The controversy doesn’t end with just Phillips’s anti-’woke’ political and cultural views, though. In recent months, his decades-long friendship with Peter Mandelson – the disgraced British diplomat and politician whose lengthy relationship with Jeffrey Epstein sparked a full-blown political scandal and his own arrest in February – has raised questions for Phillips. Questions that he’s essentially brushed aside.

Saying that he has not cut off Mandelson amid the revelations of his close ties to the deceased sex predator, Phillips merely said nobody is “perfect” while defending his longtime friend, who once served as best man at his wedding.

“What would that say about me as a person if I suddenly decided to unperson somebody because of what I discovered about them?” he said in February.


Meanwhile, as Mehdi observed on Twitter at the time, Phillips continued to cover his friend even as authorities began investigating Mandelson for misconduct. “Given Mandelson was best man at Trevor Phillips’s wedding, and they have been friends and political allies for 50 years, shouldn’t Phillips recuse himself from all Mandelson coverage at Sky News? What am I missing here?” Mehdi wondered.

Still, it is Phillips’s views on race and Muslims that have some CBS News staffers expressing concern and outrage over the latest new face that Weiss has welcomed into the Tiffany Network.

“What an utter joke. His anti-Muslim views are clearly a qualifier,” one senior CBS staffer told me, while a network insider stated that “most people I’ve talked to are just disgusted with the hire.”
According to one CBS News reporter, the poaching of Phillips from Sky News is a data point that tells a bigger story when it comes to Weiss’s editorial vision for the network.

“The hiring of Trevor Philips just further cements what’s really going on here. Bari Weiss has no interest in providing space for different viewpoints she doesn’t ideologically agree with. CBS News already has a history of a lack of inclusion. Right now, you can count on one hand, maybe half a hand, how many people with editorially prominent positions are of Arab or Muslim descent,” the reporter said.

“Trevor Philips has been accused on various occasions of Islamophobia and anti-Muslim statements and attitudes,” the reporter continued. “I think what we’re seeing here is Bari looking to utilize a Black man who is one of the very few minorities that have been hired since she took over – but he’s not just a minority, he’s a minority that doesn’t believe in multiculturalism and says ‘Muslims are different.’”

Representatives for CBS News, Weiss, and Phillips did not respond to requests for comment.

After spending over $14 million on no-bid contracts to coat the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool with “American flag blue” waterproofing paint, Donald Trump has relentlessly congratulated himself for making the pool “beautiful” after other presidents had left it “filthy” and “dirty” for years.

Unfortunately, as soon as the project was completed, algae returned in force and turned the water green – prompting MAGA to go into desperate spin mode. According to Real America’s Voice host Grant Stinchfield, the president could be the victim of foul play ahead of America’s 250th birthday bash.

“President Trump fixes the reflecting pool and a week later it’s green again, loaded with algae… Sabotage… Vandalism? I believe it is. The left can’t stand Trump, American greatness and his quest to make DC beautiful again,” he tweeted.

Other right-wing luminaries, meanwhile, attempted to claim that pictures and videos of the dirty pool were faked by “TDS sufferers genuinely hate America.” As for Fox, it was probably summed up best by Jesse Watters’s lackey Johnny Belisario, who merely waved off the algal invasion as anti-Trump noise.

“I’m here at the newly renovated reflecting pool. It’s painted American flag blue,” he said on Monday night. “The Democrats will tell you there’s green algae. There’s pool guys cleaning it up. No other president would do that.”

While “MAGA hacks” like Benny Johnson gush over the announced U.S.-Iran peace agreement as a “historic” American victory, the hawks that make up Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ aren’t nearly as jubilant – largely because the president is refusing to release any text of the agreement until after it’s signed on Friday.

“[W]hy can’t we, the people, see the damn MOU? Not through people briefed by an anonymous person. Honestly, I’ve never seen anything like this,” Fox host Mark Levin, who has advised Trump on the war effort, tweeted this week.

Fox anchor Dana Perino, a former George W. Bush administration official, grumbled about the secrecy over the agreement, adding that Trump boasts of having the “most transparent administration in history.”

“There is talk of a reconstruction fund of hundreds of billions of dollars,” Fox contributor Marc Thiessen, who has also had the president’s ear throughout the war, groaned. “That will be like doing the Marshall Plan for Germany while the Nazis were still in power. I’m anxious to see what the details of the deal are and what gets negotiated, but I’m concerned.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘ Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

item.title: Fascists and Football: How Italy's Mussolini and Argentina's Junta Turned the World Cup Authoritarian
item.description: “History repeats itself,” people often say in reference to politics and current affairs. But the saying also applies to sports. While Trump’s authoritarian agenda is certainly bringing chaos to the World Cup, he isn’t the first fascist to do this. In this second episode of Zeteo’s three-part video…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
“History repeats itself,” people often say in reference to politics and current affairs. But the saying also applies to sports. While Trump’s authoritarian agenda is certainly bringing chaos to the World Cup, he isn’t the first fascist to do this.

In this second episode of Zeteo’s three-part video series, Mehdi goes back in time to two famous World Cup competitions that were plagued by authoritarian regimes:

  • Italy’s

    1934 World Cup, which took place during the height of dictator Benito Mussolini’s fascist regime and his country’sgenocidein Libya

  • Argentina’s

    1978 World Cup, which was held during Argentina’s “Dirty War,” in which the military regime forciblydisappearedtens of thousands of Argentines and tortured their people in detention centers just kilometers away from the World Cup stadium

“So many Argentines had a sibling or a parent or a child who had just been picked up off the street and was gone,” Argentine-American NPR journalist Jasmine Garsd says in the episode. “Sportswashing works for a moment…and then everyone has to go back to a life of terror. And distress.”

Mehdi and the episode’s other guests – including Human Rights Watch’s Minky Worden and Propaganda Researcher Daniele Serapiglia – also discuss the controversies around Russia and Qatar’s recent World Cup competitions and the double standards when it comes to the coverage of the U.S.’s own scandals.

Paid subscribers can watch this full episode, as well as the third one that will be released soon. They can also access the first episode here.

Free subscribers can watch a 2-minute preview. Upgrade your subscription today to access this episode and the others, paywall-free!

item.title: Trump Can’t Buy Joy
item.description: On this day in 1858, at the Illinois Republican Convention in Springfield, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln warned that amid national divides over slavery, “A house divided against itself cannot stand.” Within three years, Lincoln would be president, with civil war raging. Good morning! Prem…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1858, at the Illinois Republican Convention in Springfield, U.S. Senate candidate Abraham Lincoln warned that amid national divides over slavery, “A house divided against itself cannot stand.” Within three years, Lincoln would be president, with civil war raging.

Good morning! Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Tuesday wherever you are. I’m just returning from London, where I met Paddington, cursed the monarchy, and ate scones. One observation: It is crazy how captured US society is by crypto and AI. On the “tube,” I saw maybe one ad every now and again about our dear favorite industries. But in the New York subways, you simply cannot escape the next “.ai” or startup shoving itself in your face. I’m sure they’re on their way everywhere, but it was jarring to see the contrast even between two otherwise relatively similar cities…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the joy of sports in New York outshines Trump’s pathetic UFC 80th birthday extravaganza (which also served as one huge advertisement for trash he and his family are invested in). Plus: Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed is out with a new ad featuring one Bernie Sanders.

Could there have been a better illustration of the competing visions of society than Donald Trump’s pathetic, sad UFC extravaganza happening alongside the effervescence in New York, as thousands from around the world enjoy the World Cup, and rejoice in the Knicks’ victory?

Let me clear my throat first: There’s no need to strain ourselves to project political commentary onto sports, or to conflate only certain evils with certain sports. The NBA isn’t inherently a holier institution than the UFC, not when you recall that Knicks’ owner Jim Dolan warmly welcomed Trump to a game last week, or that Dolan has also allegedly used facial recognition technology to surveil a trans woman, lawyers, protesters, and more.

What’s of interest here to me is how the public engaged with sport, and what sport did or did not afford to society. So let’s take a look.

In one corner, we have Trump’s UFC event. A spectacle that took nearly a month, hundreds of workers, and $60 million to set the stage for. The affair was paid for, in part, by a glitzy array of sponsors that Trump and his family themselves are invested in: the Trump sons’ crypto exchange; Trump’s sad, failing social media platform; and, of course, his gaudy “Trump Coins.” (More on all this corruption later.)

In attendance were an array of top Trump officials like Pete Hegseth, Marco Rubio, Kash Patel, and JD Vance; billionaire CEOs like Meta’s Mark Zuckerberg and Paramount’s David Ellison; sports team owners including the New England Patriots’ Robert Kraft and the New York Knicks’ James Dolan; and hundreds of military service members who were “recruited” to help fill the stands.

The function was topped off by a military flyover (and what appeared to be at least one brawl between fans).

The entire thing, notably, did not seem to register beyond traditional UFC media, or obsequious right-wing media. It didn’t seem to resonate with the public beyond, which may well be a good thing for Republicans: The event was every bit as shameful and corrupt as it was an insult to American taxpayers and institutions. Perhaps the only moment that broke through from the whole affair was when fighter Josh Hokit exclaimed in the ring, to a microphone held by podcast host Joe Rogan: “Michelle Obama is a man! Am I right, America?!”

It was ugly enough that UFC honcho and Trump ally Dana White felt obligated to reject Hokit’s commentary.

Meanwhile, in another corner, we have the fervor coursing through New York City, amid the New York Knicks’ victorious run in the NBA playoffs – after more than 50 years – and the World Cup, which is being hosted in several cities across North America, including the New York and New Jersey area.

In contrast to Trump’s garish event, the revelry in New York had been open to the public: livestreams of the NBA playoffs were projected everywhere, including at city-run screens all over the city (a practice that will now continue for the World Cup).

People of every creed got in on the excitement. Basketball became the vessel for community, simply, for fun. The atmosphere wasn’t just reserved for billionaires and politicians and sponsors.

The phrase “My mayor’s Muslim, my bagel’s Jewish, my Christian Dior, Knicks in four!” (popularized first in a user’s comment on TikTok before being exploited, unfortunately, by prediction market Kalshi) went massively viral – emblematic of the confluence of optimism coursing through the city’s streets. History was being made, and everyone, everyone could be part of it – even in a city for which such a premise was hotly contested during the mayoral race just a year earlier.

And that spirit, naturally, ties beautifully into the World Cup. A global event that the United States is fortunate to host, alongside Canada and Mexico. While the Trump administration’s brutal immigration “enforcement” apparatus has put a damper on the World Cup, New York in particular serves as such a direct rebuke of Trump and his ilk’s aims. After all, whether Trump keeps thousands of fans out or not, New York itself teems with residents who hail from the nations descending upon the US.

And New York, under their Muslim Mayor Zohran Mamdani, has embraced it – low-cost tickets for the matches; soccer events across the city; extended soccer field lighting for residents; and encouraging millions of residents and visitors to travel across the city to explore the exceptionally wide range of immigrant communities and restaurants.

This week, sports in the U.S. presented two competing worlds. In one corner: We see sports as exclusive, gaudy, jingoistic thumping – not even for America, per se, but for one man who represents the worst of it. In the other, we see sports as a vehicle for a more international patriotism – for optimism, and interest in one another, and in fun being for all, and not for the few.

If nothing else, where would you rather be? The 80th birthday party for the guy who was best friends with the world’s most infamous pedophile? Or the streets of New York City, dancing and singing and chanting and having hope and meeting a new friend who’s at once from oceans away, but who, just like you, was in the city too?

  • Signs and wonders:Trump’s deal with Iran wassignedelectronically – on the U.S. side by an administration that hasclaimednon-personal signing is invalid – before a formal event in Switzerland on Friday.
  • Term limits:In France, for theG7, Trumptalked upthe deal. Terms were not disclosed, butReutersreporteda U.S. official saying they included immediate opening of the Strait of Hormuz, ending the U.S. blockade, and economic relief for Iran.Tehransaidit would charge “fees” in the Strait, not “tolls.”
  • Windfall waffle:On CBS, JD Vance wasasked: “The Iranians are saying they’re gonna have access to a $300 billion reconstruction fund. True or false?” The VP’s answer contained many words, all evasive and boilable down to… “True.” Trump on Tuesdaydeniedthe claim, calling it “ridiculous” and a “rumor.”
  • Secret cash:In Israel, it wasreportedthat before the deal was reached, the Trump administration covertly approved Iran receiving billions of dollars in return for Qatari vessels being allowed through the Strait. Trump has made payments to Iran central to his case against Barack Obama’s Iran nuclear deal from 2015.
  • Gas plunge:Oil prices fell, but after three-and-a-half months of war, the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve wasreportedto be at its lowest level since 1983.
  • Lebanon latest:Though the U.S.-Iran deal said conflict in Lebanon would cease, Israel wasn’t a party to it. One person waskilledin an Israeli strike in southern Lebanon, while an Israeli drone was reported over Beirut. Israel has killed nearly 3,800 people in Lebanon since the start of the war. Tehranwarnedthat any Israeli strike on Lebanon or its continued occupation, as Benjamin Netanyahu has promised, will constitute a violation of the agreement.
  • ‘Not happy’:Trumpsaidtoday that he’s “not happy with the way Israel has handled themselves with Lebanon and with Hezbollah.” He laterclaimedthat he suggested to Israel to let Syria “take care” of Hezbollah. “[T]o be honest with you, I think they’d do a better job of doing it.”
  • ‘Not all Hezbollah’:In an apparent moment of some form of clarity, Trumpsaidthat “too many people are being killed” in Lebanon and criticized Israel for striking entire residential buildings: “You don’t have to knock down an apartment house every time you’re looking for somebody because there’s a lot of people in those apartment houses – and they’re not all Hezbollah.”

Donald Trump’s long-promised White House UFC fight might’ve been staged in part as a presidential birthday party, in part as a patriotic pageant, but contrary to White House denials, it also amounted to one long ad for businesses from which the first family and friends derive great profit. Here’s a look at how:

TKO Holdings: Donald Trump purchased up to $50,000 of stock in TKO Holdings, the parent company of UFC, in advance of the White House event.

World Liberty Financial: The Trump family’s crypto exchange, run by Donald Trump Jr and Eric Trump, gave $125,000 worth of its USD1 stablecoin to two champions, Justin Gaethje and Ciryl Gane. Gaethje was the night’s biggest earner with $825,000 in total. One crypto expert told The Guardian the payout amounted to a giant ad for the Trump family token. At the event, an announcer

Truth Social: The deeply unprofitable Trump social media platform sponsored the heavyweight match between Josh Hokit and Derrick Lewis. “Truth Social… the real voice of President Trump,” the announcer declared. Trump is the company’s largest shareholder.

Trump Coins: Trump Coins – not to be confused with the president’s crypto “meme coin,” and not a new grift for the president – sponsored closed captioning on the UFC broadcast. A website for the coins, which bear Trump’s likeness, describes them as “the official gold and silver of UFC Freedom 250.” A special edition 1oz gold version made for the event sold for $11,999.99, a markup of more than $7,000 over the melt value of the gold it contained.

Polymarket: Donald Trump Jr. advises the prediction market, whose logo lined the cage. His father’s Commodity Futures Trading Commission chair, Mike Selig, the sole sitting member, is fighting to shield Polymarket from state regulators, having until last year worked at a law firm that represents the market.

Crypto.com: The night’s co-presenter (and funder of a $1 million crypto bonus pool) is a Trump business partner, building prediction markets into Truth Social and backing a planned Truth Social exchange-traded fund. Its parent has given $35 million to Trump’s super PAC this election cycle.

Abdul El-Sayed, the progressive candidate for the Democratic nomination for U.S. Senate in Michigan, is out with a new campaign ad.

Titled “Chorus” and made by Fight Agency – started by veterans of campaigns for Senators Bernie Sanders, Ruben Gallego, and John Fetterman – the ad is backed by an initial six-figure spend. Polling shows a tight race between El-Sayed and AIPAC-backed Rep. Haley Stevens, with state Senator Mallory McMorrow also in contention.

  • Political probe:Gavin Newsomsaidhe and his wife were being investigated by Donald Trump’s DOJ, because the California governor is “considering running for president.” Citing prominent figures targeted by the administration, including James Comey and Letitia James, Newsom said, “Today, I proudly join that list.”
  • By the book:New York Timesreporters Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swanrevealedmore behind-the-scenes White House conversations in a new excerpt of the forthcoming bookRegime Change, this time on how Trump aides debated suspending legal rights in response to anti-ICE protests in Minnesota this year.
  • Rally plan:Trumpsaidhe would turn a July 4 250th anniversary event on the National Mall into “the most spectacular Trump rally of them all.”
  • Senate news:Republicans havealreadybooked about $100 million in ads against Graham Platner, the Democratic U.S. candidate in Maine. In Alaska, in a win for incumbent Republican Dan Sullivan, a candidate with the same name wasruledineligible.In Montana, Democrat Alani Bankheaddeclinedcalls to drop out and back independent Seth Bodnar against Republican Steve Daines.
  • Media matters:TheWall Street Journalsaidthe Trump DOJ closed a probe of Paramount’s bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, to head off opposition to the deal. Foxagreedto buy streamer Roku in a $22 billion cash-and-stocks deal.
  • GOP disgrace:A Muslim Republican was told to “convert or leave” the Texas GOP convention in Houston, theTexas Tribunereported. Other Muslims also reported hostility.
  • Pet horror:An LA familymournedJameson, a 2-year-old, Knicks-jersey-wearing dog, shot dead by cops amid celebrations of New York’s NBA title win.

UFC fighter Josh Hokit’s transphobic comment about Michelle Obama on Sunday night was not his first such disgrace. Which sports star did he target after a fight in January?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel killed four Palestinians on Monday, officialssaid. Two victims, a medic and his son, were killed in a rooftop strike. The death toll from Israeli strikes since a so-called “ceasefire” last October is now nearly 1,000.
  • 🇺🇦 🇷🇺 Russia strikes:Drones and missiles hit Kyiv and Kharkiv, killing 11 and setting a cathedral alight. With G7 leaders set to discuss the war, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskycalledfor a “decisive and substantive” response.

  • 🇨🇴

    Polls pause:ELN, the largest remaining rebel group in Colombia,announceda unilateral ceasefire ahead of next weekend’s presidential election.

  • 🇸🇩

    Drones toll:More than 1,000 have been killed by drones in Sudan this year, the UNsaid, adding that sexual violence was “rampant” in the war-torn country.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:DR Congoreporteda jump in new cases, with 72 reported in 24 hours, 29 fatal, bringing the caseload to 782 and the death toll to 181. A Doctors Without Borders official said the outbreak was “outpacing the response effort.”


Florida GOP gubernatorial candidate Byron Donalds spoke about the importance of childhood literacy from a podium emblazoned with… a spelling error. In the words of one observer, that’s some “Veep level shit.”

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald contributed to this newsletter.
