item.title: '60 Minutes' Correspondent Was Working on Francesca Albanese Profile When Bari Weiss Fired Her
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we bring you a scoop about a story that Cecila Vega was working on for ‘60 Minutes’ when she was…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we bring you a scoop about a story that Cecila Vega was working on for ‘60 Minutes’ when she was fired. Plus: David Ellison and Co. try to put out Bari Weiss’s fires, who is (or isn’t) watching Stephen Colbert’s replacement, and Fox soothes Trump’s fragile ego after the Madison Square Garden boos.

One of the stories that Cecilia Vega had lined up for the upcoming season of ‘60 Minutes’ when she was unceremoniously fired by Bari Weiss, according to multiple sources familiar with the matter, was a profile on Francesca Albanese – the UN special rapporteur on the Occupied Palestinian Territories.

The story, which had been approved by the show’s then-senior leadership this spring, was still in its early stages at the time of Vega’s termination, and the correspondent had yet to sit down with Albanese for an interview – which was scheduled for the end of this month.

In recent weeks, the segment’s production staff and writers had already conducted background reporting on the UN official and met with Albanese in Rome and Genoa to film b-roll footage for the story. The program was also in the middle of setting up Vega’s shoot with Albanese, only for that to be postponed indefinitely following the “Black Thursday” firings at ‘60 Minutes’ late last month.

“Yes, she was working on a profile of me for ‘60 Minutes,’” Albanese told Zeteo when reached for comment. “In May, her crew traveled to two different locations to follow my work, and we had two additional segments planned: an interview in Tunis, where I live, and another piece before I present my report to the UN General Assembly.”

As of now, according to network sources, the story is on hold since there is no correspondent currently assigned to it following Vega’s dismissal. There are several other stories that were meant for Vega that are also now in limbo.

Albanese has found herself on the receiving end of withering criticism from the Israeli government, the Trump administration, and supporters of Israel for documenting the genocide in Gaza and advocating for legal action against Israel. The U.S. government imposed sanctions on her last year, which a federal judge found violated her First Amendment rights. While she was briefly removed from the sanctions list following that decision, the Trump administration added her back after an appeals court issued an administrative stay on the lower court’s ruling.

I have learned that one of the subjects Vega was interested in exploring with Albanese was how the sanctions have impacted her and her family’s lives, especially that of the UN expert’s eldest daughter, who is an American citizen.

Weiss – an avowed “Zionist fanatic” – has also used her anti-woke, pro-Israel site The Free Press to take aim at the official.

In an editorial last fall, The Free Press’s editors dismissed a scholar association’s resolution accusing Israel of genocide because one of its sources was Albanese, who they claimed was “discredited,” saying it was “another reason not to trust the ‘experts.’”

So it’s fair then to raise an obvious question – did this upcoming story about a loud and vocal critic of Israel’s treatment of Palestinians play a part in Vega’s dismissal?

Vega and representatives for CBS did not respond to requests for comment.

Vega said in a memo after her termination that she and her producing teams had “experienced efforts to insert political bias into our stories.” She added that the show’s reporters had “held back on submitting story pitches about important news topics out of fear of the internal repercussions.”

Amid the “shitshow” engulfing ‘60 Minutes’ over the mass firing of the show’s senior management and several correspondents, Vega’s termination has been the one that has left many scratching their heads. Not only was she the youngest of the show’s seasoned reporters, having just joined in 2023 from ABC News, but her contract wasn’t due to expire until March 2027.

Yet, while Sharyn Alfonsi had long been expected to be let go due to her public run-ins with Weiss over the “Inside CECOT” story, and Scott Pelley was fired following his heated confrontation with Weiss’s handpicked new ‘60 Minutes’ boss Nick Bilton, a target may have been placed on Vega’s back even before Weiss was hired.

Months before David Ellison closed his merger with Paramount and eventually installed Weiss as CBS News’s chief editor, a ‘60 Minutes’ story by Vega over the way the Biden administration was handling the war in Gaza sparked loud complaints from not only the Anti-Defamation League and other pro-Israel groups, but also Shari Redstone – the company’s then-owner and a passionate supporter of Israeli causes.

That January 2025 story, which centered on ex-State officials resigning in protest over the U.S. policy in Gaza, prompted Redstone to demand immediate action – eventually leading to the resignations of top CBS News leaders over concerns of corporate interference. This came as ‘60 Minutes’ was already under intense pressure due to Trump’s meritless lawsuit. (We know how that ended.)

With the Ellisons and Weiss also being extremely fervent supporters of Israel, network staffers and insiders I’ve spoken to have openly wondered if that Gaza war story was the impetus behind Vega’s firing.

Since arriving at the network last fall, many of Weiss’s personnel and programming decisions have at least appeared to be based on pro-Israel sentiment. Whether it’s making Tony Dokoupil the face of ‘CBS Evening News,’ hiring an Israeli-flag-waving correspondent, firing a well-respected bureau chief for supposedly running her office like a “Hamas cell,” laying off the network’s leading Gaza reporter after Mike Huckabee complained about her, or allowing Benjamin Netanyahu to choose which reporter interviews him, critics have ample evidence to accuse her of pushing an Israel First agenda at CBS.

At the same time, there could be another reason Weiss targeted Vega, at least according to one senior CBS News staffer.

“If you look at The Free Press, or if you look at any of Bari’s own thoughts on it, she would just look at Cecilia Vega as some DEI hire and not as an extraordinarily talented journalist who rose through the ranks at ABC News and hit the highest heights of America’s number one legacy news network and who is so good, she was recognized by ‘60 Minutes,’ which is the number one news broadcast show,” the staffer said.

“Cecilia is extraordinarily gifted, talented, and qualified, and so I just feel like, as well as whatever her perception of her potentially being pro-Palestinian, or whatever it is, I also think the bigger issue is that Bari just doesn’t think women of color deserve to be in those spaces,” the staffer added.

While we may never fully know why Vega was let go, we will keep an eye on ‘60 Minutes’ this upcoming season to see if the Albanese profile makes it to air.

Days after Bilton vowed to protect the show from corporate interference in a conciliatory memo, prompting the program’s last remaining correspondents to stick around, Lesley Stahl revealed to ‘60 Minutes’ staffers – during a champagne toast meant to boost morale – that Ellison promised her he’d respect the editorial independence of the iconic newsmagazine while apologizing for all the recent turmoil.

Of course, folks at CBS News have heard this before from Ellison. He made that exact promise in August after he fully took over Paramount, only to immediately install a pro-Trump ombudsman and foist Weiss on the network, leading the staff to quickly lose trust in him.

Still, whether he’s being genuine or not, it’s apparent that Ellison realizes just how much of a fire-alarm fire Weiss started with her haphazard overhaul of ‘60 Minutes,’ which has only led to wall-to-wall negative headlines – like Pelley telling the New York Times that Weiss is incompetent and putting her thumb on the scale for Trump. Or Alfonsi and Vega

According to Status’s Oliver Darcy, Weiss has been holed up in a secure bunker within CBS headquarters while Ellison and other Paramount bigwigs look to clean up the mess she created. All the while, rumors continue to swirl that, with the Warner Bros. deal fast approaching, Paramount brass is looking to find a veteran media executive to share the duties of running a combined CNN-CBS News with Weiss.

However, the rub – at least based on the media spin coming out of Paramount – is that Ellison wants Weiss to maintain editorial and creative control while having another person handle the business-side operations. What seasoned executive in their right mind would cede editorial duties to someone who has done nothing but spark dumpster fires these past eight months?

‘Comics Unleashed,’ the low-budget comic talk show that replaced Stephen Colbert’s ‘The Late Show’ on CBS’s late-night schedule, is actively repelling viewers after just two weeks in its new time slot.

According to Nielsen data, the program – which has been around in syndication for over 20 years – averaged just 764,000 total viewers and 114,000 in the key 25-54 advertising demographic last week. This is down from the previous week, which saw ‘Comics Unleashed’ draw a nightly average of 849,000 viewers.

Comparatively, ‘The Late Show’ averaged 2.7 million viewers a night during its final season, and pulled in a massive audience of 6.7 million for its finale – which came months after Paramount canceled the long-running franchise.

While the company said it axed the MAGA-skewering Colbert’s show “purely for financial reasons” and alleged the top-rated program lost $40 million annually, Ellison’s merger with Paramount was approved by the Trump administration just days after the cancellation was announced.

Byron Allen, the host and producer of ‘Comics Unleashed,’ appears to be unfazed by the show’s terrible ratings. The stand-up comic turned mogul, whose media company owns the program, is paying Paramount $15 million to run the show during Colbert’s airtime in exchange for advertising revenue.

“CBS has won big-time because they have zero production costs and now they are saving $55 million a year,” he told the Los Angeles Times this week.

Leave it to Trump’s favorite morning show to obediently tell its Dear Leader that the Knicks fans were actually shouting “Boo-urns” when they showered the president with jeers during his Madison Square Garden appearance at Game 3 of the NBA Finals.

After Fox’s social media channels told its followers the crowd chanted “USA!” with “President Trump in attendance,” and its digital outlet merely reported that the arena reacted “to Trump far differently than college football fans,” ‘Fox & Friends’ got right to work on Tuesday morning.

With a chyron blaring “Trump Gets Mixed Reception From Fans,” Brian Kilmeade – a member of Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ – insisted that the president “did get a good response” while comparing the crowd’s reaction to a Mike Tyson fight.

“I would say it was mixed,” Kilmeade proclaimed to viewers – and one in particular. “There were people cheering. I’ll challenge anybody on that – I was sitting right there.”

As if the North Korea vibes weren’t already on the nose, ‘Fox & Friends’ even did a segment on how the “aggressive rhetoric” coming from Kim Jong Un has quieted down, thanks to Donald Trump – only to then jump directly into a discussion about the Knicks game.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

item.title: 'He Didn’t Carry a Weapon': Israel Is Killing Lebanon's Medics at an Unprecedented Rate
item.description: BEIRUT, Lebanon – Zeinab Qassir had been preparing for her brother’s wedding. Hussein, 37, was weeks away from getting married when, on the morning of May 22, he woke to the sound of an explosion near his home in the southern Lebanese village of Deir Qanoun. A Syrian family had been struck while on…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
BEIRUT, Lebanon – Zeinab Qassir had been preparing for her brother’s wedding.

Hussein, 37, was weeks away from getting married when, on the morning of May 22, he woke to the sound of an explosion near his home in the southern Lebanese village of Deir Qanoun. A Syrian family had been struck while on a motorbike. He learned there were injured people. He dressed in his paramedic gear and moved toward the site.

Zeinab tried to stop him. She had seen a drone circling low over the area.

He went anyway.

“I held him before he left,” she tells me, “without knowing it was the last time I would see him alive.”

A short time after paramedics arrived at the site, a woman filming from her window captured the moment a second strike hit their ambulance.

Zeinab ran toward the scene.

“When I arrived, Hussein was still alive,” she says. “There were victims on the ground around me. I didn’t see any of them. All my attention was on my brother as he took his final breaths.”

“Hussein loved ambulance work since he was a child. He didn’t carry a weapon,” says Zeinab. “He woke up from sleep because he heard there were civilians who needed help, and he went to save them.”

Israel’s targeting of medical personnel in Lebanon is not new. But the scale is.

Since March 2, when Israel re-invaded southern Lebanon, near-daily attacks on first responders have left at least 131 paramedics killed and more than 385 wounded. At least 36 centers have been hit, and 169 vehicles have been damaged, according to Lebanon’s Health Ministry.

Their work, especially in the southern part of the country, is increasingly important, as Israel continues to ramp up attacks. Since the latest escalation began, Israeli strikes have killed more than 3,665 people and wounded at least 11,321, with over a million displaced from the south, where Israel has burned entire villages to set up a so-called buffer zone.

Among the dead healthcare workers, about one in five were killed after the supposed “ceasefire” took effect in April, according to World Health Organization figures.

Joud Suleiman was 15 years old.

He had been coming to the Nabatiyeh ambulance center since he was 5 or 6, trailing the paramedics and helping them carry food parcels and bread to families in the area. By the time the latest hostilities broke out, he had been embedded in the rhythms of the team for most of his life.

His father, Mohammed Suleiman, leads the southern city of Nabatiyeh’s ambulance unit. When strikes intensified, he tried more than once to send his son to Beirut. Joud refused each time.

“He said, ‘I’ll come back by taxi if you throw me out of Nabatiyeh’,” Mohammed recalls. “This is my decision, and I’m staying to help the paramedics with you.”

Because of his age, Joud wasn’t deployed as a field medic. He handled logistics: preparing food supplies, readying equipment, running support for the teams in the field. On the morning of March 24, he and colleague Ali Jaber set out on a motorcycle marked with ambulance insignia and emergency lights and headed to a community kitchen called “Beit al-Zaman al-Jamil” – or the “House of the Beautiful Old Days” in English – to prepare meals for crews working the area.

An ambulance vehicle was roughly 200 meters (650 feet) ahead of them. Then a drone appeared over the same road.

“It never crossed my mind that Ali and Joud could be the targets,” Mohammed says. “They were known to everyone as paramedics. Everything they were wearing, everything with them, confirmed their humanitarian identity”.

He called Joud’s phone. No answer. Others tried Ali. Nothing.

When Mohammed reached the site, the motorcycle was burning. Both young men were dead. Ali was in his blue ambulance uniform. Joud was wearing his jacket and helmet, the ambulance insignia visible on both.

“It was the first strike we faced on a Nabatiyeh ambulance – the first time we confronted the targeting of paramedics this way,” Mohammed says. “The shock was severe. They lost two of their closest friends.”

The funerals were held. Then the team went back to work.

“We buried our martyrs and returned to the field,” Mohammed says. “What happened will not push us to retreat. It will make us hold tighter to the mission they died for.”

The attacks have not been limited to moving vehicles. Mohammed Shakroun, the civil defense training official at the Islamic Risala Scout Association, a prominent Lebanese youth and humanitarian organization, described ambulances hit while responding to prior strikes, crews targeted while evacuating the wounded, stationary vehicles destroyed at their centers, and the centers themselves reduced to rubble.

There have been “more than 70 direct targeting incidents involving paramedics, centers, and ambulances in the south and Jabal Amel,” Shakroun tells me, “and between 50 and 60 indirect incidents – strikes near work positions, or grenades dropped from drones on crews mid-mission.”

In Nabatiyeh, the Risala Association had around 150 paramedics at the start of the latest hostilities, but only 20 remain – some have been killed, while others have been wounded or displaced and unable to work. At the same time, the group there is operating with only five ambulances after Israeli attacks put roughly 30 out of commission.

Nationwide, the Risala Association began with approximately 1,500 trained emergency and civil defense personnel. The numbers operating in the most exposed zones are now a fraction of that.

Falah Faqih, who heads the Civil Defense center in Nabatiyeh, says 12 of his personnel have been killed and 42 wounded. Nine centers in the region have been completely destroyed.

He spoke with difficulty about what it means to keep working.

“The duty of the call remains the most important,” he tells me, describing the staff and volunteers he lost as his children – including his sister’s son. “They are all my children. They are all one soul.”

The Lebanese Red Cross has lost two volunteers in direct strikes. Youssef Assaf died on March 9 from wounds sustained during a rescue operation in Majdal Zoun. He had four children, the youngest just a few months old. Hassan Badawi was killed on April 12 when a drone struck him as he stepped out of an ambulance with a stretcher during a rescue mission in Bint Jbeil, leaving, according to his colleagues, 300 pieces of shrapnel in his body. He had a pregnant wife and a son at home.

In both cases, the Red Cross said it had shared the coordinates and details of the missions with all parties to the conflict through standard deconfliction channels, including coordination with the UN peacekeeping mission in Lebanon (UNIFIL). The ambulances were fully marked with the Red Cross emblem, lit, and visible from all sides.

“This is why the Red Cross volunteers hug each other and say goodbye before every mission,” says Ali Saad, a liaison coordinator with the Lebanese Red Cross.

The day after Badawi was killed, an Israeli airstrike hit the Red Cross center in Tyre while a wounded person was being transferred, killing him and damaging several ambulances.

The Israeli military has not directly responded to the growing evidence that it targets medical personnel, which is considered a war crime. In mid-March, Israeli military spokesperson Avichay Adraee issued a statement accusing Hezbollah of using medical facilities and ambulances for military purposes. No evidence was provided, and the Lebanese Ministry of Health denied the claim.

Ramzi Kaiss, a Human Rights Watch researcher for Lebanon, says the organization has investigated multiple cases and found no indication that the medical vehicles or facilities struck were being used for military purposes.

The attacks have included “stationary ambulances, fixed ambulance centers, and the homes of paramedics,” Kaiss explains. And they’ve hit a range of relief organizations, meaning the attacks can’t be explained by affiliation with any single party, Kaiss adds. “This weakens the Israeli justifications considerably.”

Ali Sweidan, a legal analyst at The Legal Agenda, notes that the “warnings” Israel sometimes provides do not meet the standards required by international humanitarian law before a protected facility can be struck. General statements issued to medical crews, he argues, cannot replace specific, direct warnings with adequate time to respond.

“The goal appears to go beyond targeting a vehicle or a specific mission,” Sweidan says. “It extends to paralyzing the entire rescue and relief infrastructure in the south.”

Doctors Without Borders, which has been working alongside civil defense teams across Nabatiyeh, warned that the fear of repeated strikes – known as double-tap strikes – has paralyzed the rescue response itself. The response is delayed, or ambulance crews spend only minutes at blast sites. Rescues are delayed. Excavation equipment goes unused. People have been left under rubble for hours – sometimes days.

“We have treated patients whose conditions were critically worsened by these delays,” the organization noted, “including severe trauma cases who later died from their injuries.”

Despite the known risks, first responders continue to put their communities first. It is why Hussein Qassir ignored his sister’s pleas not to go out on that fateful day. It is also why Mohammed Suleiman keeps going out – because someone has to. For these men, it’s duty against everything else, and duty wins, every time, even when everything else includes the people they love most.

The same day Mohammed Suleiman’s son and colleague were buried, his team was back out distributing food aid.

“The most painful thing,” says Suleiman, “is not only losing my son, but that Joud was a child who chose to spend his days serving people and helping those in need.”

Ali Awada is a Lebanese journalist who has covered Lebanese affairs for more than a decade for regional and international outlets.

This story has been produced in collaboration with Egab.

item.title: Is There a Double Standard for World Cup Criticism?
item.description: How beautiful will the beautiful game be this summer, when the World Cup has already been mired by exorbitant ticket prices, a criminal complaint against FIFA’s new president, and of course, entry and visa chaos caused by U.S. President Donald Trump’s vicious crackdown on Middle East and African…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
How beautiful will the beautiful game be this summer, when the World Cup has already been mired by exorbitant ticket prices, a criminal complaint against FIFA’s new president, and of course, entry and visa chaos caused by U.S. President Donald Trump’s vicious crackdown on Middle East and African countries? And are there double standards in the way Western media reacted to recent world cups versus this one?

In this new three-part, deep-dive video series, Zeteo’s editor-in-chief and lifelong Liverpool and England football fan Mehdi Hasan sets out to answer those questions.

Mehdi goes back in history to explain how some of the world’s most shameful leaders have used the World Cup and FIFA’s corrupt behavior to raise their own status on the world stage and also distract from their country’s heinous human rights violations – a practice known as sportswashing.

In this first episode, Mehdi starts off with an authoritarian whose sportswashing is not getting nearly enough attention right now: U.S. President Donald Trump – one of the co-hosts of this summer’s World Cup, despite his administration having threatened to bring rogue ICE agents to the World Cup stadiums.

Throughout this series, Mehdi is also joined by a team of expert guests, like former football player turned political scientist Jules Boycoff, Human Rights Watch Global Initiatives Director Minky Worden, and New Yorker Global Affairs Columnist Ishaan Tharoor.

Trust us when we say, you do NOT want to miss out on this new series from Zeteo!

Paid subscribers can watch this full episode, as well as the two others that will be released soon. Free subscribers can watch a 3-minute preview. Upgrade your subscription today to access this episode, paywall-free, as well as the other two episodes still to come!

item.title: 🦪 Graham Platner Steamrolls in Maine – and Scorches the Media
item.description: On this day in 1967, a UN-brokered ceasefire ended the Six-Day War between Israel and its Arab neighbors. Having doubled in size, Israel refused to withdraw from occupied regions, including Gaza, with terrible consequences for Palestinians that continue to this day. Good morning! Andrew here,…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1967, a UN-brokered ceasefire ended the Six-Day War between Israel and its Arab neighbors. Having doubled in size, Israel refused to withdraw from occupied regions, including Gaza, with terrible consequences for Palestinians that continue to this day.

Good morning! Andrew here, writing from the great state of Maine, where Graham Platner is now the Democratic Senate nominee and bashing the media for coming after him instead of trying to understand the movement he’s building.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Platner moves on to face Susan Collins in what should be the premier race of the 2026 midterms, the one that will be its ugliest and likely most expensive. I hope you like politics. Elsewhere, the war in Iran is getting hotter, and Republican lies about “voter fraud” in California are only getting worse.

As Graham Platner won Maine’s Democratic Senate nomination easily on Tuesday, the Marine combat veteran and oyster farmer torched the national media that’s trying to destroy him.

“The national pundits, the political establishment, they keep looking for that one story, that one headline, that one moment in my life that they can define the campaign by,” Platner said. “But in trying so hard to understand me, they fail to understand that this is not about me at all. This is a movement about us – about the far too many, working far too hard and struggling far too much, at the hands of the ruling class.”

Zeteo’s Prem Thakker broke the news of Platner’s Senate bid last summer. (Watch Mehdi’s interview with him here, and read one of mine.) I’ve followed Platner’s insurgent campaign closely on the ground throughout Maine, as he’s held town hall after town hall, discussing his views, the problems voters are facing and the oligarchs robbing them, and his plan to organize to build worker political power.

A first-time candidate for office, the populist, anti-war, anti-genocide Democrat is set to face five-term Republican Senator Susan Collins in the fall. The race, which could determine control of the Senate, is sure to be a blistering one. Democrats have never beaten Collins. This year, she’ll run in an exceedingly difficult environment for Republicans: Donald Trump is deeply unpopular, as is his unending war in Iran, which has sharply raised energy costs.

Platner offered a fiery preview of his general-election campaign against Collins Tuesday night, describing her as “spineless and corrupt.” He accused her of helping funnel federal contracts to her husband, a longtime lobbyist. He asserted that Collins had gotten “21 times wealthier just in the last 15 years,” adding: “Susan Collins is getting rich while we’re getting screwed.”

Voters should support him, he said, “if you want to stop war with Iran and end the forever wars, if you want to give workers the raise they deserve [and] seniors the security they worked for, if you want to bring back Roe v. Wade.”

It was clear for many months that Platner was on track to win the Senate nomination – even before Maine’s Democratic governor, Janet Mills, suspended her campaign – despite him being confronted with a series of scandals that might traditionally nuke a candidate’s political career.

There were the decade-old misogynist Reddit posts, for which he apologized. It was revealed he had a skull-and-crossbones tattoo, dating back to his time in the Marines, that resembled a Nazi symbol; he denied knowing what it meant and got it covered up. More recently, there was the revelation that Platner had sexted other women early in his marriage – news leaked by his former political director, who quit his campaign as the controversies began in October.

Last week, Lyndsey Fifield, a right-wing operative Platner dated between 2013 and 2015, came forward alleging that Platner had been physically intimidating with her at times, which he denied, and toxic and crude overall. The national media has used the story to press Democrats to condemn Platner, with some conservative Democrats calling for him to drop out.

Less covered: Fifield once led an effort called “Ladies for Kavanaugh” defending then-Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh when he faced allegations of sexual assault (which he denied). Fifield was also apparently the source who anonymously told journalists that Platner had described his skull-and-crossbones tattoo as “my Totenkopf.”

Even as the negative news coverage mounted, the only question Tuesday was how much Platner would win by. As of this morning, he had 72% of the primary vote.

While Maine voters have now selected Platner as their nominee, expect conservative Democrats to keep slamming him, in hopes that he will cede the party’s nomination. (The deadline to do so would be mid-July.)

Speaking with CNN on Tuesday, Rep. Josh Gottheimer, a pro-Israel Democrat who finished fourth with 12% in New Jersey’s race for governor last year, called on Platner to “get off the ballot and let another Democrat step in,” citing Fifield’s allegations.

Democratic Senate leaders issued a statement backing Platner Tuesday evening. Senate Dems’ super PAC put out a video touting him. The candidate was full speed ahead.

“Susan Collins doesn’t serve us,” he said in his victory speech. “She serves Donald Trump, she serves the Epstein class, she serves her corporate donors and the corrupt political system that has rigged the economy against us. She does not serve us. And so we will defeat Susan Collins. We will take back the Senate seat. We will take back our power.”

In other election news:

  • Maine’s gubernatorial races and the Democratic 2nd congressional primary are

    headedto ranked-choice run-offs. The Dem primary for governor is close.

  • Billionaire progressive Tom Steyer

    concededin the California governor's race, which took place last week. Democrat Xavier Becerra and Republican Steve Hilton advance.

  • Randy Villegas

    advancedin a California congressional primary with support from Bernie Sanders. He’ll face Republican Rep. David Valadao.

  • Warmongering Republican Senator Lindsey Graham

    wonhis primary in South Carolina.

  • Republican Rep. Nancy Mace

    finishedfifth in the South Carolina primary for governor, done in by her vote to release the Epstein files in defiance of Trump.
  • Chopper shocker:Donald TrumpsaidIran downed a helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz, which meant the U.S. “must, of necessity, respond to this attack.” Two aviators were rescued using unmanned boats, U.S. forces said.
  • Point counter point:Iransaidit did not deliberately bring the Apache down, while U.S. outlets reported a collision. Trump told theWall Street Journalthe downing of the helicopter “wasn’t a big deal.” Then strikes began.
  • Strikes time:U.S. Central Commandsaid, “The mission is a proportional response to unjustified Iranian aggression.” Iranian sources told theNew York Timesthat military installations were targeted, including in Bandar Abbas and Qeshm Island.
  • Strikes back:Iranian forcessaidmore than 20 attacks were launched on U.S. military personnel in the region, including a base in Jordan and the Fifth Fleet in Bahrain, and a U.S. drone was shot down over Iran. Kuwait said it was “intercepting hostile aerial targets.”
  • Talking points:Amid it all, theTimesreportedU.S. and Iranian negotiators were close to agreeing on four issues: suspension of uranium enrichment, diluting enriched uranium, dismantling nuclear sites, and snap inspections. JD VancetoldCBS a deal “could happen in the next week, but… could also happen months from now.”
  • Lebanon latest:Israelkilledat least six in the southern Lebanese city of Tayr Debba today, as the Israeli military targeted several areas across the south. Heavy fighting between Israel and Hezbollah was alsoreported. As attacks continued, Lebanon’s Health Ministry said the toll was now at least 3,666 dead, 11,188 wounded, and more than a million displaced.
  • Crisis fuel:U.S. authorities said global oil consumption was expected to fall by more than a million barrels a day, thanks to the war. The government also said U.S. airlinesspent$6 billion on jet fuel in April, up a huge 78% on last year.

Early in his interview with ‘Meet the Press’ last weekend, before he flew off the rails, Donald Trump heard the dreaded “q” word three times.

He agreed with his 2016 self, that the Middle East was a “quagmire.” But his illegal war on Iran, he said, would not become a “quagmire” – because U.S. forces were “not going to be there” and because he had “Just wiped out the military of a very dangerous country. And I wiped out the nuclear threat.”

By Sunday night, the Iranian military, evidently not wiped out, was exchanging missiles with Israel. Talks for a deal to contain Iran’s nuclear program, evidently not wiped out, dragged on.

And then, as tends to happen with quagmires, it all got quaggier still.

U.S. forces were definitely there. On Monday evening, an Apache helicopter went down off Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz. By Tuesday, the two airmen were rescued, and Trump said the U.S. would strike back, accusing Tehran of downing the helicopter.

It was reported that the Apache went down after colliding with a drone, and that it had not been determined if the collision was intentional. The U.S. launched strikes anyway. Footage showed explosions and plumes of fire. Iran said it launched more than 20 strikes on U.S. targets in response.

Iran has repeated that Israel must cease attacks on Lebanon, if there is to be peace. In southern Lebanon, Israel continued its onslaught. Many deaths were reported.

In Tehran, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi told the U.S.: “Leave our region if you want to be safe.”

Back home, even Christian talk radio hosts were calling the war a quagmire. JD Vance was dragged in, too. Speaking to USA Today to promote his new book, the Veep was obliged to insist: “It’s not going to become a quagmire.”

It all seemed pretty quaggy from here.

  • ICE boost:House Republicanspassed$70 billion for ICE and Border Patrol by a party-line vote, 214-212. The Marshall Project and MS NowsaidICE had detained more than 500 babies and toddlers under Donald Trump.
  • ‘Circular firing squad’:Retiring Senator Thom Tillis blasted fellow Republicans for blocking his bill to eliminate Trump’s “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund and pushing the SAVE America voter suppression bill, Punchbowl Newsscooped.
  • California scheming:Republican Steve Hilton advanced to the gubernatorial election, despite Trump’sdire predictions. Regardless, Trump-fueled GOP claims of voter fraud continued, with one federal prosecutor promising charges but admitting he had no evidence. “I know people have theories and they have ideas,” Bill Essaylisaid. “What we need right now are witnesses.”
  • Corruption watch:ProPublicadetailedhow an Indian billionaire “secured major policy wins from the Trump administration,” while paying at least $100 million to an obscure Texas oil start-up linked to Donald Trump Jr.
  • Minnesota move:JD Vancesaidhe was referring Minnesota Governor Tim Walz and AG Keith Ellison to the DOJ for prosecution over social services fraud in the state.
  • Tariff grab:Trump is trying to avoid repaying billions in illegally collected tariffs,Politicoreported. The Supreme Court struck down the tariffs in February.
  • Social Security crisis:Payments will be cut from 2032 unless action is taken to shore up the program, trustees warned. AARP CEO Myechia Minter-JordantoldtheNew York Times: “This should be a wake-up call: Congress needs to act.”

On Tuesday, former Rhode Island Governor and Biden Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo backed off a 2028 White House run. Which rather more confrontational sport did the Democrat play at college, later calling it “good training” for politics?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:The Palestinian Health MinistrysaidIsrael was killing hospital patients by denying the ability to go abroad for treatment. UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres called on Israel to reopen all crossings into the territory, and “ensure the rapid, safe and unhindered passage of humanitarian assistance at scale throughout Gaza.”
  • 🇵🇸 Ethnic cleansing:Amnesty InternationalaccusedIsrael of accelerating an ethnic cleansing campaign against Palestinians in the occupied West Bank. “What we are witnessing is deliberate, state-led annexation, in complete violation of international law, unfolding before the eyes of the entire world,” Amnesty said.

  • 🇵🇰🇦🇫

    Renewed fighting:AfghanistanaccusedPakistan of killing 14 people, including 11 children, in the first strikes after a period of relative calm along the border. Pakistan said it targeted militant hideouts and killed more than two dozen militants, perAP.
  • 🇬🇧 Anti-immigrant violence rocks Belfast:Masked mentorcheda bus and burned immigrant families out of their homes after authorities charged a Sudanese man with attempted murder in connection with a stabbing attack in Belfast on Monday.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:The death toll in DR Congopassed100, amid attacks on aid workers and armed conflict. In Kenya, police fired tear gas to disperse protesters near a military airbase where the U.S. plans to build an Ebola quarantine facility.


In which a man who saw Donald Trump call his wife ugly and claim his dad was linked to JFK’s killer, then endorsed Trump anyway, decides to attack Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico for lacking “masculinity.” Righto, Ted. You do you.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.
